title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Probe Detection Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting probe detection issues on the Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system.

steps.title: Verify Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the probe is fully seated in the correct port. Check for any dirt, debris, or corrosion on the probe connector or system port. Why: Loose or dirty connections are the most common cause of detection failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Cable
steps.instructions: Look for visible damage such as cuts, kinks, or fraying. Gently straighten any bends and ensure strain relief is intact. Why: Damaged cables can interrupt communication signals to the system.

steps.title: Swap Ports (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: If the system has multiple probe ports, try connecting the probe to a different port. Why: Determines if the issue is the probe or the system port.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Probe
steps.instructions: Connect a different, verified working probe to the same port. Why: Confirms whether the issue lies with the original probe or the system electronics.

steps.title: Check System Software and Firmware
steps.instructions: Verify that the system software supports the probe type being used. Look for any recent updates or alerts from Siemens Healthineers regarding probe compatibility. Why: Software mismatches can cause detection errors even when hardware is functional.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled system shutdown and restart. Why: Resets system communication and can clear temporary faults.

steps.title: Document Observations
steps.instructions: Record probe type, port used, error codes, and any patterns noticed. Why: Documentation aids in future troubleshooting or escalation.

CCR.Complaint: No Probe Connected message on the ACUSON Sequoia system.
CCR.Cause: Probe cable or connector may be loose or damaged; system may not be recognizing the probe.
CCR.Resolution: Verified connections and cable integrity, swapped probe port, tested with known-good probe, restarted system. If problem persisted, system and/or probe labeled Out of Service and sent for service evaluation.

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Poor Image Quality or Artifacts
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system experiencing poor image quality, streaking, shadowing, or other artifacts.

steps.title: Confirm the Probe Selection and Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct probe is selected in the system. Check that the probe is fully seated in the connector and locked. Why: Loose connections can cause intermittent signals or artifacts.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Cable
steps.instructions: Visually check for cracks, bent pins, or frayed cables. Swap with a known-good probe if available to see if the issue persists. Why: Damaged probes are a frequent source of poor image quality.

steps.title: Check the Imaging Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the preset, depth, gain, and focus settings match the exam type. Reset to default imaging settings to rule out user-adjusted parameters. Why: Incorrect settings can mimic hardware artifacts.

steps.title: Verify the System Display and Monitor
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor or display connections are secure. Check brightness, contrast, and monitor calibration. Why: Display issues can appear as image artifacts.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System
steps.instructions: Turn off the system completely, wait 30 seconds, and restart. Why: Temporary software or memory glitches can cause artifacts.

steps.title: Check Environmental and External Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient area is free from strong electromagnetic interference. Check for nearby devices like cautery units or mobile phones. Why: External interference can affect image acquisition.

CCR.Complaint: Images are fuzzy with intermittent streaks; probe appears connected but artifacts persist.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the image artifact persisted with multiple probes and after adjusting settings; display and cable connections verified; external interference ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Siemens Healthineers for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Probe types tested and serial numbers
Image presets and settings used during testing

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - System Boot Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia systems that fail to boot or freeze during startup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never attempt troubleshooting on a system in active patient use. Move patients to an alternative imaging system first. Ensure safe environment before performing any electrical or software tests.

steps.title: Verify Power Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is plugged into a live, grounded outlet. Check for illuminated power indicators on the system. Why: Many boot failures are caused by loss of AC power or poor grounding.

steps.title: Check Power Cables and Surge Protection
steps.instructions: Inspect AC power cord for damage or loose connections. Bypass extension cords or surge protectors temporarily to test. Why: Faulty cables or overloaded surge protectors can prevent startup.

steps.title: Check Battery Backup (If Present)
steps.instructions: Some ACUSON Sequoia systems have internal batteries; verify charge status. Replace or remove battery and attempt boot with AC power only. Why: A failing internal battery can halt boot processes.

steps.title: Confirm Peripheral Devices
steps.instructions: Disconnect all non-essential peripherals (external drives, USB devices, printers, probes not needed at startup). Attempt boot with minimal configuration. Why: A failing peripheral can block or freeze the boot sequence.

steps.title: Observe Error Messages or LED Codes
steps.instructions: Document any displayed error codes or warnings. Check the system display for BIOS or software initialization messages. Why: Error codes provide guidance on the root cause.

steps.title: Soft Reset / Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off the system completely. Wait 1–2 minutes, then power on. Why: Clears temporary faults that can block boot.

steps.title: Network and Software Checks
steps.instructions: Disconnect from network if errors appear during network initialization. Ensure the system is running supported software versions if a recent upgrade was performed. Why: Network or software conflicts can delay or prevent boot.

CCR.Complaint: System will not complete boot; remains on manufacturer logo.
CCR.Cause: External power verified; all peripherals disconnected; system still fails to boot.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Siemens Healthineers service for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Error codes or LED behavior documented
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Touchscreen or Control Panel Issues
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound systems with unresponsive or erratic touchscreen and control panel behavior.

steps.title: Verify Power Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is properly plugged into a tested, live electrical outlet. Check for loose or damaged power cords. Why: Low or intermittent power can cause control electronics to behave erratically.

steps.title: Check System Boot and Status Lights
steps.instructions: Observe startup indicators and error messages. Confirm the system completes booting without alerts related to control panel or touchscreen. Why: Some touchscreen or panel issues appear only during or after boot errors.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen and Control Panel Surface
steps.instructions: Clean the screen and buttons with approved non-abrasive cleaner. Remove any protective covers or films that could interfere with capacitive touch. Why: Debris or moisture can prevent proper input registration.

steps.title: Test Input with Alternate Method
steps.instructions: If available, connect a mouse or use keyboard shortcuts to navigate menus. Observe if control issues persist when bypassing touchscreen or panel. Why: Helps isolate whether the problem is the display/panel or the main system.

steps.title: Check for Loose or Damaged Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect ribbon or connection cables between the touchscreen/control panel and main system. Re-seat cables if accessible without disassembly. Why: Intermittent contact can mimic failure.

steps.title: Perform a System Restart
steps.instructions: Power down the system completely, wait 30–60 seconds, then power on. Observe if touchscreen responsiveness improves. Why: Temporary software glitches can affect input responsiveness.

steps.title: Verify Software Updates and Calibration
steps.instructions: Check if the system requires touchscreen calibration (if available in menu). Confirm system software is up to date. Why: Software inconsistencies can affect panel behavior.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen and control panel buttons intermittently unresponsive.
CCR.Cause: Observed touchscreen and panel do not register input; no debris or loose cords found; system software up to date.
CCR.Resolution: Device labeled Out of Service; sent for bench evaluation with Siemens Healthineers service.

helpfulDetails: Startup indicators and error messages noted.
Screen and panel cleaned and visually inspected.
Final device status: Out of Service pending repair.

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - DICOM/PACS Transfer Failures
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system DICOM/PACS transfer failures, focusing on external network and configuration checks.

steps.title: Verify Network Connectivity
steps.instructions:
Ensure the ultrasound system is properly connected to the hospital network.
Check Ethernet cable connections, Wi-Fi if applicable, and network port lights.
Why: Network connectivity is a common cause of PACS transfer failures.

steps.title: Ping the PACS Server
steps.instructions:
Use system network tools to ping the PACS server IP.
If the server is unreachable, note the network error.
Why: Confirms whether communication is blocked at the network level.

steps.title: Check DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions:
Verify the AE Title, IP address, and port of the PACS server in the Sequoia DICOM settings.
Ensure there are no typos or misconfigurations.
Why: Incorrect DICOM settings prevent proper image transfer.

steps.title: Confirm PACS Server Availability
steps.instructions:
Check if the PACS server is online and accepting connections.
Coordinate with the IT department if necessary.
Why: Even with correct settings, the PACS server must be operational.

steps.title: Test with Another Modality or Workstation
steps.instructions:
If possible, send a test image from another system to the same PACS destination.
Why: Helps isolate whether the issue is with the Sequoia or the PACS/network.

steps.title: Review System Logs
steps.instructions:
Check the Sequoia DICOM transfer logs for error codes.
Note recurring messages or patterns.
Why: Logs often indicate the source of failure (network timeout, server refusal, etc.).

steps.title: Restart DICOM Service or System
steps.instructions:
Restart the Sequoia DICOM service or perform a system reboot if external checks pass.
Why: Clears temporary software or communication issues.

CCR.Complaint: Sequoia ultrasound system cannot send images to PACS; error ‘DICOM connection failed.’
CCR.Cause: Network connectivity verified; PACS server reachable; Sequoia DICOM configuration checked—settings correct; logs show repeated timeouts.
CCR.Resolution: Escalated device to vendor for internal software/communication evaluation; system labeled Out of Service; alternative imaging modality provided for patient exams.

helpfulDetails: PACS server status at time of failure
Attempts from other devices to same PACS destination

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sample Aspiration Failure or Clot Detection Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers experiencing sample aspiration failures or clot detection errors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never troubleshoot a blood gas analyzer while it is actively being used for patient testing. Ensure clinical staff have access to another functioning analyzer before beginning troubleshooting or maintenance.

steps.title: Verify the device is powered on and fully initialized
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer has completed startup and is not in standby, calibration, or maintenance mode. Sample aspiration will not occur if the instrument is performing automated processes.

steps.title: Confirm the sample type and container are correct
steps.instructions: Verify that the syringe or capillary tube used is compatible with the RAPIDPoint sampling system. Improper containers or adapters may prevent proper aspiration.

steps.title: Inspect the sample for clots or improper mixing
steps.instructions: Blood gas samples must be properly mixed after collection. Small clots or inadequate mixing can trigger clot detection or block aspiration. Gently roll the syringe if the sample appears separated.

steps.title: Check the sample probe area for visible contamination or obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the probe tip and sample port for dried blood, debris, or residue that could restrict sample uptake.

steps.title: Perform the analyzer’s automated probe cleaning function
steps.instructions: Use the system’s maintenance menu to run a probe rinse or cleaning cycle. This often clears minor buildup within the probe.

steps.title: Verify cartridge installation and status
steps.instructions: Confirm the reagent cartridge is correctly installed and not expired. Some aspiration errors occur when the cartridge fluid pathway cannot support proper sample flow.

steps.title: Check waste and fluid levels
steps.instructions: Ensure the waste container is not full and that system fluids (wash or reagent fluids) are available. Improper fluid levels can interrupt aspiration cycles.

steps.title: Run a quality control (QC) test
steps.instructions: Running QC can help determine if the aspiration pathway is functioning normally. If QC samples aspirate successfully, the issue may be related to the patient sample.

steps.title: Inspect tubing and sample pathway connections (external areas only)
steps.instructions: Without opening the analyzer, visually check accessible tubing and connections for kinks, disconnections, or leaks that may affect sample flow.

steps.title: Restart the analyzer
steps.instructions: A controlled reboot can reset fluid handling routines and clear temporary aspiration or sensor errors.

CCR.Complaint: User reported the RAPIDPoint 500 analyzer would not aspirate patient samples and repeatedly displayed clot detection errors.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found dried blood residue on the sample probe and evidence of poor sample mixing from multiple syringes.
CCR.Resolution: Performed probe cleaning cycle, cleaned accessible probe area, and verified proper sample handling with staff. Analyzer returned to normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any alarms or error messages displayed
Final device status (returned to service or removed from service)

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sensor Cartridge Recognition or Installation Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for situations where a Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 or 500e blood gas analyzer fails to recognize a sensor cartridge or reports installation errors.

steps.title: Confirm the analyzer is not actively processing a patient sample
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is idle before troubleshooting. Interrupting an active process may create additional system errors or corrupt cartridge data.

steps.title: Verify the correct sensor cartridge type is installed
steps.instructions: Confirm the cartridge model matches the analyzer configuration and is within its expiration date. Incorrect or expired cartridges may fail recognition during initialization.

steps.title: Check cartridge installation and seating
steps.instructions: Open the cartridge compartment and confirm the cartridge is fully seated and latched according to manufacturer design. Improper seating can prevent electrical contacts from aligning with the analyzer.

steps.title: Inspect the cartridge contacts and connector area
steps.instructions: Remove the cartridge and visually inspect the electrical contact area for dust or debris, moisture or condensation, and physical damage to contacts. If debris is present, gently clean the area using approved lint-free materials and avoid using excessive force or liquid.

steps.title: Reinstall the sensor cartridge carefully
steps.instructions: Reinsert the cartridge and ensure it locks into place securely. Watch the display for initialization messages confirming cartridge detection.

steps.title: Restart the analyzer
steps.instructions: If the cartridge is still not recognized, perform a controlled power cycle by turning the analyzer off, waiting approximately 30 seconds, and powering the analyzer back on. This allows the system to reload cartridge configuration and communication.

steps.title: Observe system messages during startup
steps.instructions: During boot-up, monitor the screen for cartridge recognition messages or system error codes. These can help determine whether the analyzer is detecting the cartridge but rejecting it.

steps.title: Check for physical damage to the cartridge
steps.instructions: If recognition errors persist, inspect the cartridge for cracked plastic housing, bent or missing contact pins, or fluid leakage. If any of these conditions are present, the cartridge should not be used.

steps.title: Try a known-good sensor cartridge
steps.instructions: If available, install another cartridge that is known to be functional and within expiration date. If the analyzer recognizes the second cartridge, the original cartridge is likely defective.

steps.title: Review system alerts or maintenance logs
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer's alert history or maintenance logs for cartridge recognition errors or related system faults that may indicate a recurring issue.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the RAPIDPoint 500 analyzer displayed a 'Sensor Cartridge Not Recognized' error after replacing the cartridge.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cartridge was not fully seated in the cartridge bay, preventing proper contact with the analyzer connector.
CCR.Resolution: Removed and reinstalled the sensor cartridge, ensuring it was fully seated and latched. Analyzer successfully recognized the cartridge and returned to normal operation.

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Automatic Calibration Failures or QC Lockouts
description: Guide for troubleshooting Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers reporting automatic calibration failures or QC lockouts.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Not Currently in Use
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is not actively processing a patient sample. Calibration or QC troubleshooting should only be performed when the instrument is idle to avoid interrupting clinical workflows.

steps.title: Review the On-Screen Error Message
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer display for specific calibration or QC error messages. Note whether the issue involves: Calibration failure, QC failure, QC overdue or lockout, Sensor drift or instability. This information helps determine whether the issue is related to the sensor cartridge, QC material, or analyzer conditions.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Cartridge Installation and Status
steps.instructions: Check the installed sensor cartridge for proper seating in the cartridge bay, correct installation date, and expiration status. A cartridge nearing expiration or recently installed may temporarily produce calibration errors until the system stabilizes.

steps.title: Check Calibration Pack and Reagent Status
steps.instructions: Review reagent and calibration pack levels in the analyzer menu. Confirm packs are not empty or expired, reagent tubing is properly connected, and no kinks or disconnections are present. Low or expired reagents commonly cause calibration failures.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample and QC Ports
steps.instructions: Look for dried blood, debris, or residue around the sample port or QC aspiration area. If contamination is present, carefully clean the area according to hospital policy and ensure no obstruction prevents proper aspiration. Blocked ports can interfere with calibration and QC sampling.

steps.title: Verify QC Material Condition
steps.instructions: If the issue involves QC lockout, confirm the QC ampoules or cartridges are within expiration, ensure QC material has been stored according to manufacturer recommendations, and verify the correct QC level is being used. Improper QC material handling can cause repeated QC failures.

steps.title: Confirm Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Blood gas analyzers are sensitive to environmental factors. Verify that room temperature is within normal operating range, ventilation openings are not blocked, and the device is not exposed to excessive heat or cold. Temperature instability can affect sensor calibration.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer
steps.instructions: If all external checks appear normal, perform a controlled restart of the analyzer and allow the system to complete its initialization and calibration sequence. Temporary software conditions can sometimes cause calibration interruptions.

steps.title: Observe the Next Calibration Cycle
steps.instructions: After restart, monitor the next automatic calibration cycle. Confirm that calibration completes successfully and no additional QC lockout messages appear. If calibration succeeds, the issue was likely temporary or environmental.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer reports repeated automatic calibration failures and displays QC lockout message preventing patient testing.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cartridge approaching expiration and QC material found expired during inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced expired QC material and allowed analyzer to complete successful automatic calibration. QC run passed and analyzer returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Analyzer restarted and calibration cycle observed

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sample Path Blockage or Fluidics Pump Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting sample aspiration failures caused by blockages or fluidics pump issues on RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e analyzers.

steps.title: Verify Power and Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is fully powered on and has completed its initialization sequence. A device not properly warmed up may fail to aspirate or trigger fluidics errors.

steps.title: Check Sample and Tubing
steps.instructions: Confirm that the sample syringe, capillary, or tube is properly loaded and not expired or clotted. Inspect tubing for kinks, pinches, or bends that could obstruct flow. Replace the sample line or cartridge if there is visible contamination or blockage.

steps.title: Inspect the Fluidics Pump Area
steps.instructions: Visually check the pump mechanism for obstructions such as debris, coagulated sample, or dislodged tubing. Ensure tubing is fully seated in the pump channels.

steps.title: Run a Manual Aspirate / Flush Test
steps.instructions: Use the device’s maintenance menu to run a manual sample aspirate or flush. Observe whether the device successfully moves fluid through the system. If the sample fails to aspirate, note any specific error codes displayed.

steps.title: Check Consumables and Reagents
steps.instructions: Ensure the sensor cartridge or reagent pack is correctly installed and not empty or expired. Misalignment or low reagent levels can mimic pump failure symptoms.

steps.title: Verify Sample Path Sensors
steps.instructions: Inspect the optical or pressure sensors along the fluidics path for dirt, moisture, or misalignment. Clean sensors carefully according to external maintenance procedures—do not disassemble internal components.

steps.title: Swap Cables and Connectors (if applicable)
steps.instructions: For analyzers with removable fluidics modules, reseat connectors and ensure there are no loose connections.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer reports “Sample path blockage” during routine blood gas analysis.
CCR.Cause: Sample line observed kinked and fluidics pump could not aspirate sample; flush test failed.
CCR.Resolution: Tubing replaced, pump visually inspected, manual flush attempted; persistent failure—unit labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Sample line condition (kinked, clotted, or correct)
Final device status (Out of Service, sent for repair)

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – LIS / Network Communication Failures
description: Guide to troubleshoot Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers that cannot transmit results to LIS or hospital network.

steps.title: Verify Network Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is physically connected to the network via Ethernet or verified Wi-Fi. Check that cables are secure and undamaged. Confirm the correct port and switch connection. Why it matters: Loose or damaged network cables are a common cause of LIS communication failures.

steps.title: Check Analyzer IP Configuration
steps.instructions: Navigate to the network settings on the analyzer touchscreen. Confirm the IP address, subnet mask, and gateway match the network requirements. If using DHCP, verify the device has obtained an address. Why it matters: Incorrect network configuration prevents the analyzer from reaching the LIS.

steps.title: Ping Test
steps.instructions: Use a networked computer or the analyzer’s diagnostic menu to ping the LIS server or network gateway. Successful pings indicate network connectivity is present. No response may indicate a network issue, firewall block, or switch port problem.

steps.title: Verify LIS Server Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm the LIS server is online and accepting connections. Coordinate with IT to check if any server maintenance or network restrictions are in place. Why it matters: If the server is down, communication failures are expected and not device-related.

steps.title: Check LIS Interface Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer’s LIS interface parameters (protocol, port, destination) match those configured in the LIS. Confirm the correct device ID or unit number is being used. Why it matters: Mismatched parameters can block data transmission even on a healthy network.

steps.title: Inspect Software and Firmware
steps.instructions: Verify that the analyzer software/firmware is up to date. Review any logs for communication errors or failed transmissions. Why it matters: Outdated software or firmware can cause intermittent LIS connectivity issues.

steps.title: Reboot Analyzer and Network Devices
steps.instructions: Power-cycle the RAPIDPoint analyzer and network switches if external checks pass. Re-test communication. Why it matters: Temporary network glitches or device errors can be resolved with a controlled reboot.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer fails to transmit results to LIS; ‘communication error’ displayed on touchscreen.
CCR.Cause: Network cable disconnected and analyzer could not ping LIS server.
CCR.Resolution: Secured Ethernet cable, verified IP configuration, rebooted analyzer. Communication successfully restored.

helpfulDetails: Network cable type and port verified
Analyzer IP address and subnet configuration
LIS interface settings (protocol, port, device ID)

title: Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series - Gantry Initialization Failure / System Will Not Start
description: Troubleshooting guide for Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series CT scanners that fail to initialize the gantry or will not start during system boot.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the CT table before beginning troubleshooting. If the issue occurred during a scan, safely remove the patient from the system. Move the patient to another imaging modality if needed before continuing.

steps.title: Verify the System Has Proper Power
steps.instructions: Confirm that the CT system console, gantry, and associated power cabinets are powered on. Check for a powered workstation or console, verify gantry indicator lights are illuminated, confirm there have been no facility power interruptions, and check UPS units are functioning normally if installed.

steps.title: Check for an Engaged Emergency Stop
steps.instructions: Inspect all emergency stop buttons in the CT room including the gantry front panel, operator console area, and room wall emergency stop. Ensure none are engaged. If one is pressed, reset the E-stop and restart system initialization.

steps.title: Confirm All Room Doors and Interlocks Are Closed
steps.instructions: Ensure the CT room door and any safety interlocks are properly closed and functioning. Check door sensors, verify radiation room interlock indicators, and review system messages indicating interlock faults.

steps.title: Inspect Gantry Area for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the gantry opening and patient table for objects contacting the gantry, table collision conditions, table extended too far into the gantry, or patient positioning devices interfering with movement. If needed, retract the patient table and retry initialization.

steps.title: Verify Table System Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient table is functioning normally. Check for table movement errors, verify the table is homed or parked correctly, and inspect the table control panel for errors. If the table is not in the expected position, move it to the home or park position and restart initialization.

steps.title: Check Cooling System Status
steps.instructions: Verify the CT cooling system is functioning properly. Inspect chiller status indicators, check for cooling alarms, confirm adequate room temperature, and verify cooling water flow if applicable.

steps.title: Review System Error Messages on the Console
steps.instructions: Check the operator console for specific startup or initialization error messages including gantry drive errors, rotation errors, communication errors, or detector initialization faults. Record these messages for documentation and potential service escalation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart
steps.instructions: If no external problems are found, shut down the CT system through the console, wait several minutes for subsystems to fully power down, and restart the system according to normal startup procedure.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Network Connections
steps.instructions: Check visible communication connections between the console, gantry cabinets, and system network for loose Ethernet cables, disconnected interface cables, or damaged connectors.

CCR.Complaint: CT scanner would not start this morning. System stuck during gantry initialization.
CCR.Cause: Emergency stop button on gantry was engaged, preventing gantry startup.
CCR.Resolution: Reset emergency stop, restarted system, and confirmed successful gantry initialization and normal operation.

title: Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series - Patient Table Will Not Move / Position Error
description: Guide for troubleshooting patient table movement and position errors on a Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series CT scanner.

steps.title: Verify the System Is Fully Initialized
steps.instructions: Ensure the CT system has completed startup and gantry initialization. If the system is still booting or initialization failed, table movement may be disabled until the system reaches ready status.

steps.title: Check the Table Control Panel
steps.instructions: Attempt to move the table using the table control panel or operator console. Confirm table controls respond, buttons are not physically stuck, and no error messages appear on the console. If the console allows movement but the table does not respond, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the full travel path of the table. Look for equipment carts, IV poles, linens or patient straps caught in the rails, debris or dropped objects near the base. Even small obstructions can trigger a safety stop or position error.

steps.title: Verify Emergency Stop Is Not Activated
steps.instructions: Check the CT system emergency stop buttons. Confirm no emergency stop is engaged and all E-stop buttons are fully released. An engaged emergency stop disables table motion for safety.

steps.title: Confirm Table Weight and Position Limits
steps.instructions: Verify that the patient weight and positioning are within system limits. Check for excessive patient weight, patient positioned too far forward or backward, and accessories improperly installed. If the system detects unsafe conditions, it may prevent table movement.

steps.title: Inspect Table Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Visually inspect accessible cables between the patient table and the gantry or base. Look for loose connectors, pinched cables, or visible damage. Table movement depends on communication and power connections between the table and system controller.

steps.title: Attempt Table Re-Zero or Reposition
steps.instructions: If available from the system interface, attempt to reset the table position, return the table to home position, or reinitialize table positioning. Minor encoder or position errors may clear with a reposition command.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Table System
steps.instructions: If safe to do so and no patient is present, shut down the CT system according to procedure. Wait 30–60 seconds. Restart the system and allow full initialization. This can clear temporary communication or positioning errors.

steps.title: Observe for Mechanical Symptoms
steps.instructions: During attempted movement, observe for grinding or clicking noises, jerky or partial movement, or table attempting movement but stopping immediately. These symptoms may indicate a mechanical drive issue or motor problem.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the CT scanner patient table would not move when attempting to position the patient. The system displayed a table position error.
CCR.Cause: Inspection revealed the emergency stop button on the gantry had been activated, preventing table movement.
CCR.Resolution: Released the emergency stop, verified normal table movement using console controls, and confirmed the CT system returned to normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any noises or partial movement observed

title: Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series - X-Ray Tube Overheat or Cooling System Fault
description: Guide for troubleshooting X-ray tube overheat or cooling system faults on a Siemens SOMATOM Series CT scanner, focusing on external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Check the Power and System Readiness
steps.instructions:
Ensure the CT scanner is properly powered on and all emergency stops are released.
Verify that the main power supply and UPS are functioning correctly.
Why: Inadequate power can prevent proper operation of tube cooling systems.

steps.title: Inspect Cooling System Connections
steps.instructions:
Check that all external coolant lines and connectors are securely attached.
Verify that there are no visible leaks or kinks in the tubing.
Why: Loose connections or leaks can prevent proper circulation of the cooling medium.

steps.title: Verify Coolant Levels (if applicable)
steps.instructions:
Confirm that the cooling reservoir or tank is at the correct level according to manufacturer guidelines.
Why: Low coolant levels can trigger overheat warnings.

steps.title: Check Cooling Fans and Vents
steps.instructions:
Inspect the scanner’s external cooling fans for proper operation.
Ensure vents are unobstructed and clean from dust or debris.
Why: Blocked airflow reduces heat dissipation, increasing tube temperature.

steps.title: Review Recent Scan Workload
steps.instructions:
Determine if multiple high-dose scans were performed consecutively.
Allow time for cooling between scans if necessary.
Why: Excessive consecutive scans can temporarily exceed tube cooling capacity.

steps.title: Monitor System Alarms and Logs
steps.instructions:
Check the operator console or service logs for specific error codes.
Record the frequency and conditions of the overheat warnings.
Why: Helps determine if the issue is sporadic, workload-related, or persistent.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Test Scan
steps.instructions:
With patient safety in mind, perform a low-dose test scan or phantom scan to monitor tube temperature and cooling response.
Observe whether the alarm triggers under controlled conditions.
Why: Confirms whether the cooling system is functional during normal operation.

CCR.Complaint: Operator reported a tube overheat warning during a routine scan.
CCR.Cause: External inspection revealed no visible leaks, correct coolant levels, fans functioning, but alarms persisted after multiple low-dose test scans.
CCR.Resolution: CT scanner removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and referred to Siemens Healthineers service for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Number of scans performed prior to warning
Tube temperature readings during test scan

title: Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series - Image Artifacts (Ring Artifact / Streaking)
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot Siemens SOMATOM Series CT scanners producing ring or streak artifacts in images.

steps.title: Verify Scan Parameters and Protocols
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct scan protocol is selected. Incorrect reconstruction algorithms can produce streaks or rings. Check if previous scans on the same protocol produced similar artifacts. Why: Misconfigured settings are a common source of image artifacts.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector and Gantry
steps.instructions: Check the detector array for visible damage or obstruction. Ensure no foreign objects are inside the gantry (tools, positioning devices, cables). Why: Physical issues can cause ring artifacts due to faulty detector elements.

steps.title: Check Patient Positioning
steps.instructions: Verify the patient is centered and stable on the table. Remove metallic objects or accessories that could introduce streaks. Why: Misalignment or high-density objects create streak artifacts.

steps.title: Examine Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all detector, gantry, and power connections are secure. Swap or reseat cables if possible to rule out intermittent connection faults. Why: Loose or damaged cables can create intermittent signal errors visible as artifacts.

steps.title: Run Detector Calibration / Air Scan
steps.instructions: Perform system calibration or air scan according to standard procedures. Document calibration success or failure. Why: Detector calibration corrects for sensitivity variations that lead to ring artifacts.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates or Reconstruction Errors
steps.instructions: Confirm the system software is current. Reconstruct the image using a different algorithm or workstation to rule out reconstruction software issues.

CCR.Complaint: CT images show rings and streaks affecting diagnostic quality.
CCR.Cause: Detector calibration check failed; no visible gantry or patient positioning issues.
CCR.Resolution: Performed air scan and calibration; if unresolved, removed unit from service and scheduled vendor repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds or error messages during scan
Presence of metallic objects in the scan field

title: Siemens Healthineers SOMATOM Series - PACS / DICOM Image Transfer Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting PACS or DICOM image transfer failures from SOMATOM Series CT scanners, focusing on network and configuration issues before internal faults.

steps.title: Verify Network Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the CT scanner is connected to the hospital network (Ethernet port link lights are active). Confirm the network cable is fully seated and undamaged. Why it matters: DICOM transfer requires active network communication; loose cables or port issues are a common cause.

steps.title: Ping the PACS Server
steps.instructions: From the CT console or service workstation, ping the PACS server IP. Confirm there is a response and note any packet loss. Why it matters: Ensures network reachability; no response indicates a network or firewall issue.

steps.title: Check DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify AE Title, IP address, and port settings on the CT scanner match the PACS configuration. Confirm that DICOM destinations are enabled and active in the modality software. Why it matters: Incorrect AE Titles or ports prevent image transfer.

steps.title: Verify PACS Availability
steps.instructions: Check that the PACS server is operational and not in maintenance mode. Confirm sufficient storage space and that it is not rejecting incoming connections.

steps.title: Restart Modality Services (if permitted)
steps.instructions: Restart DICOM service or network interface on the CT console. Why it matters: Sometimes network services hang and restarting clears temporary faults.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates or Error Logs
steps.instructions: Review CT console logs for DICOM-related error codes. Note any specific transfer errors (e.g., association rejected, connection timeout).

steps.title: Swap Ports / Cables
steps.instructions: If using a wired connection, try an alternate network port and/or cable. Why it matters: Eliminates physical network faults.

CCR.Complaint: CT images from SOMATOM Series are not appearing in PACS; transfer errors observed on the console.
CCR.Cause: Network connection verified; AE Title and PACS IP correct; PACS operational; temporary DICOM service restart did not resolve issue.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner labeled Out of Service; issue escalated to Siemens service for DICOM software evaluation.

helpfulDetails: DICOM AE Title and port settings confirmed
Final device status: Out of Service, pending repair

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira Mobile X-Ray System - System will not boot or startup freezes
description: Startup failure, frozen boot screen, incomplete initialization, power issues, or software hang conditions on a MOBILETT Mira mobile X-ray system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the MOBILETT Mira from clinical use if it will not fully boot or freezes during startup. Action: Use another mobile X-ray unit for patient imaging. Do not attempt patient exams with a system that has not completed startup, self-checks, or normal readiness checks. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is transferred to a known-working unit. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray system that freezes during startup may not be able to complete exposure control, image acquisition, safety interlock checks, or system communication checks. If this resolves the clinical need, continue troubleshooting only after the unit is away from patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the unit is doing before assuming a failed computer, battery, or generator problem. Action: Power the unit on and observe the startup sequence. Document whether the issue is: No power at all. Display remains blank. System begins booting then freezes. Siemens logo or startup screen remains displayed. Touchscreen or workstation does not respond. Error message appears during startup. Unit shuts down or restarts by itself. Boot issue only happens on battery power. Boot issue only happens while plugged into AC power. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified and can be compared against power, battery, display, software, or internal system causes. If the unit boots normally after a restart and no errors return, perform a functional check before returning it to service.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Look for external conditions that could prevent safe startup. Action: Inspect the unit for: Impact damage. Loose panels. Damaged power cord. Damaged plug. Liquid intrusion. Signs of overheating. Burning smell. Unusual fan noise. Blocked vents. Debris around the base or workstation area. Expected outcome: No obvious unsafe condition is present. If there is liquid intrusion, burning smell, unusual heat, smoke, or visible electrical damage, stop troubleshooting, remove the unit from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: A poor outlet or weak AC connection can cause startup failure, charging problems, or unstable boot behavior. Action: Plug the MOBILETT Mira into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the outlet has power using an outlet tester or another known-working device, according to facility policy. Check that the power cord is fully seated at the wall and at the unit, if externally accessible. Avoid power strips or questionable extension cords. Expected outcome: The unit receives stable AC power. If the system boots normally on a known-good outlet, document the original outlet issue and notify facilities or the appropriate department.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: A damaged cord may allow partial power, intermittent charging, or unstable startup. Action: Inspect the AC cord for: Cuts. Crushed insulation. Bent plug blades. Loose strain relief. Signs of heat damage. Intermittent connection when gently moved. Expected outcome: Cord and plug are intact and safe to use. If the cord or plug is damaged, remove the unit from service until the cord issue is corrected and electrical safety can be verified.

steps.title: Allow the System to Charge Before Retesting
steps.instructions: If the battery is deeply discharged, the unit may not boot properly or may freeze during startup. Action: Connect the MOBILETT Mira to AC power. Allow the unit to charge for a reasonable period according to facility practice. Observe charging indicators or power status indicators. After charging, attempt startup again. Expected outcome: The unit powers on and completes startup normally. If the unit boots only after charging, document battery condition, run time concerns, and whether the issue repeats. If the unit still will not boot while connected to known-good AC power, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Compare Startup on AC Power Versus Battery Power
steps.instructions: Separating AC and battery behavior helps narrow the cause. Action: Attempt startup while connected to AC power. Then, if safe and the battery has sufficient charge, attempt startup on battery power. Observe whether the freeze or failure happens in one condition or both. Expected outcome: Boots on AC but not battery: suspect low battery, failed battery pack, charging issue, or battery communication issue. Boots on battery but not AC: suspect AC input, charger, power cord, or power supply path. Fails on both: suspect internal power, workstation, software, control system, or communication issue. If the issue is isolated to battery or charging behavior, document that clearly for repair follow-up.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: A temporary software hang may clear after a controlled restart. Action: Shut the unit down using the normal shutdown method if the controls respond. If the system is frozen and normal shutdown is not possible, follow facility-approved power-down practice for this model. Wait long enough for the system to fully power down. Reconnect AC power and restart the unit. Expected outcome: The system completes startup without freezing. If the unit starts normally, continue with a functional check before returning it to service. If the startup freeze returns, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Display and User Interface Response
steps.instructions: A startup issue may actually be a display, touchscreen, or workstation interface issue. Action: Observe whether the unit appears powered even if the screen is blank or frozen. Check for: Power indicator lights. Fan activity. Drive or system sounds. Backlight on the display. Touchscreen response. Keyboard, mouse, or control panel response, if applicable. Any external monitor output, if supported by facility process. Expected outcome: Determine whether the entire system is failing to boot or only the user interface is not responding. If the unit appears to power normally but the display remains blank or frozen, remove it from service and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Startup Error Messages
steps.instructions: Startup faults may point toward a failed subsystem, safety interlock, communication issue, detector problem, generator fault, or workstation error. Action: Record the exact displayed message. Do not paraphrase the error. Note when it appears: Immediately after power-on. During system initialization. During workstation startup. During detector connection. During generator readiness. After user login. Expected outcome: The exact fault information is captured for repair, vendor support, or service history review. If the message identifies a specific subsystem, troubleshoot only external and safe items related to that subsystem before escalating.

steps.title: Remove Nonessential External Accessories
steps.instructions: A connected accessory or peripheral may cause startup delay, communication timeout, or software hang. Action: Disconnect nonessential external accessories, if safe and allowed by local procedure. Check items such as: USB devices. External keyboard or mouse. Network cable, if connected. Barcode scanner. External storage device. Nonessential detector accessories. Restart the system. Expected outcome: The unit boots normally without the accessory connected. If removing an accessory allows normal boot, inspect or replace the accessory and document the finding. If the problem persists, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Detector and System Communication State
steps.instructions: Some mobile X-ray systems may delay or hang during startup if detector communication, registration, or wireless connection fails. Action: Check whether the detector is installed, charged, and properly seated or stored. Verify that the detector battery is not depleted. Confirm that any detector compartment, latch, or docking connection appears normal. Restart the unit and observe whether startup freezes during detector initialization. Expected outcome: Detector-related startup delay or communication failure is either ruled out or identified. If the unit boots after removing or reseating a detector according to facility procedure, document the detector serial number and behavior.

steps.title: Check Network Conditions if Startup Freezes During Login or Worklist Loading
steps.instructions: A unit may appear frozen if it is waiting for network, user authentication, worklist, or PACS communication. Action: Determine whether the freeze occurs before or after the operating software loads. If it freezes during login, worklist loading, or network connection: Check wireless signal availability. Confirm the correct network profile is being used. Compare with other mobile imaging units in the same area. Try booting in an area with known-good wireless coverage. Check whether other devices are having PACS, RIS, worklist, or network issues. Expected outcome: Network-related delay is distinguished from a true boot failure. If the system boots normally in a known-good wireless area, document the network location and notify the appropriate IT/PACS support team.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Freezing at the Same Startup Point
steps.instructions: A consistent freeze point usually indicates a repeatable system, software, storage, communication, or subsystem initialization problem. Action: Restart the system and observe whether it freezes at the same screen, message, or startup percentage. Record: Screen text. Error message. Time from power-on to freeze. Whether fans or indicators remain active. Whether the touchscreen responds. Whether the issue changes on AC versus battery. Expected outcome: A repeatable symptom pattern is documented for repair escalation. If the freeze happens at the same point every time, stop repeated restarts and escalate.

steps.title: Perform Basic Functional Check if Startup Completes
steps.instructions: If the unit boots successfully after troubleshooting, verify that it is safe before returning it to service. Action: Confirm: System reaches normal ready state. Touchscreen responds. User login works. Battery status is acceptable. Detector is recognized. Network/worklist connection works, if applicable. No active faults remain. Drive/brake functions are normal. Exposure readiness checks pass according to facility policy. Expected outcome: The system is confirmed ready for clinical use. If any readiness check fails, remove the unit from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Siemens MOBILETT Mira would not complete startup and remained frozen on the boot screen.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit froze during system initialization on both AC power and battery after testing a known-good outlet and checking the power cord.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the startup freeze behavior, and sent it for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit was on AC power, battery power, or both
Battery charge level or charging indicator behavior
Exact startup screen or error message
Whether the display was blank, frozen, or responsive
Whether fans, lights, or sounds were present
Whether the issue occurred before or after user login
Whether detector communication appeared during startup
Any unusual heat, smell, noise, or shutdown behavior

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira Mobile X-Ray System - Battery charging fault or poor battery runtime
description: Battery charging faults, reduced runtime, poor charge retention, docking power issues, or mobile X-ray shutdowns during normal use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the MOBILETT Mira from patient use if the battery charge is low, runtime is unreliable, or the system may shut down during imaging. Action: Use another mobile X-ray system if patient imaging is urgent. Do not attempt troubleshooting while the system is positioned for an active patient exam. Move the unit to a safe service area if possible. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not dependent on a mobile X-ray system with questionable power reliability. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray system that loses power during positioning, exposure preparation, or image handling can delay care and create safety risks.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Battery Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact complaint before assuming battery failure. Action: Ask clinical staff what was observed: Unit would not charge. Unit showed low battery after charging. Runtime was shorter than expected. System powered off during use. Charging indicator did not appear. Battery fault message appeared. Unit only works while plugged in. Expected outcome: The battery issue is clearly defined and repeatable. If the issue is only a user perception problem, such as the unit not being left connected long enough to charge, educate the user and stop.

steps.title: Check AC Power at the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Verify that the charging source is good. Action: Plug the MOBILETT Mira into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check whether the outlet is controlled by a wall switch, emergency power circuit, or power strip. Test the outlet with an outlet tester or another known-good device if appropriate. Expected outcome: The outlet supplies stable AC power. If the outlet is dead or unreliable, move the unit to a working outlet, notify Facilities if needed, document the outlet issue, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check for external charging path problems. Action: Inspect the AC power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose strain relief, or signs of overheating. Confirm the plug seats firmly into the wall outlet and the system power inlet. Check for looseness at the unit-side connection. Expected outcome: The power cord and plug are intact and secure. If the cord is damaged, remove the unit from service until the cord is replaced or evaluated. Do not continue charging with a damaged cord.

steps.title: Confirm Charging Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe whether the system recognizes AC power. Action: Connect the unit to AC power. Look for charging indicators, power LEDs, screen messages, or battery status changes. Allow the system several minutes to detect AC power and begin charging. Expected outcome: The system shows that AC power is connected and charging is active. If there is no AC recognition, continue checking the external charging path before suspecting internal charger failure.

steps.title: Verify System Power Mode and Shutdown Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the problem is charging-related or startup/runtime-related. Action: With the unit connected to AC power, power the system on. Observe whether it boots normally. Disconnect AC only when safe and observe whether the system remains powered by battery. Expected outcome: The unit operates normally on AC and remains stable on battery. If the unit only works on AC power and immediately shuts down on battery, suspect depleted, failed, disconnected, or end-of-life battery condition after external checks are complete.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charge Time
steps.instructions: Rule out a deeply discharged battery. Action: Leave the unit connected to a known-good outlet for an appropriate charging period. Avoid repeatedly powering the system on and off during recovery charging. Recheck the battery status after charging. Expected outcome: Battery percentage or runtime estimate improves. If the battery does not recover after adequate charging time, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Heavy Use or Workflow-Related Runtime Loss
steps.instructions: Determine whether the runtime complaint matches usage conditions. Action: Ask whether the unit was used continuously, left unplugged overnight, driven long distances, or used for repeated exposures without charging. Check whether the unit is normally parked near a working outlet. Expected outcome: Runtime concern is separated from normal battery drain. If the issue is caused by workflow, educate the area to keep the system plugged in when parked and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for External Damage or Environmental Concerns
steps.instructions: Look for signs that the power or battery system may be unsafe. Action: Check for unusual heat, burning smell, fluid leakage, damaged covers, swollen battery access panels, impact damage, or abnormal fan noise. Expected outcome: No signs of overheating, battery damage, or electrical hazard are present. If heat, smell, leakage, smoke, or visible battery damage is found, unplug the unit if safe, remove it from service, label it Out of Service, and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Restart the System Safely
steps.instructions: Clear a possible temporary charging status or software reporting issue. Action: Power down the system normally. Leave it connected to AC power. Restart the unit and recheck battery status and charging indication. Expected outcome: Battery status and charging indication return to normal. If the charging fault clears and runtime is normal after charging, document the finding and return the unit to service after normal safety checks.

steps.title: Check for Battery Status Messages or Error Codes
steps.instructions: Record any system messages before escalation. Action: Look for battery, charger, power supply, or charging-related messages on the display. Document the exact wording, error code, when it appears, and whether the unit is on AC power or battery. Expected outcome: Any fault information is captured accurately for repair follow-up. If a battery or charging fault remains active after external checks, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Runtime Check if Safe
steps.instructions: Only perform this if the system is not needed for patient care and the battery is not showing unsafe symptoms. Action: Fully charge the unit if possible. Operate the system in a controlled service area. Monitor how quickly the battery level drops. Do not perform unnecessary exposures unless your facility procedure allows it and proper radiation safety controls are in place. Expected outcome: Battery runtime is consistent with expected use. If runtime is clearly poor, drops suddenly, or the unit shuts down unexpectedly, remove the unit from service and escalate for battery/charger evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Siemens MOBILETT Mira would not hold a charge and powered down during portable imaging rounds.
CCR.Cause: Verified known-good outlet and power cord, but the unit showed poor battery recovery and reduced runtime after charging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MOBILETT Mira from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented battery behavior, and sent the unit for bench evaluation of the battery and charging system.

helpfulDetails: Battery percentage before and after charging
Whether unit operates on AC power
Whether unit shuts down immediately on battery
Any battery or charger fault messages
Unusual heat, smell, sound, or visible damage
Whether issue occurred after long unplugged storage

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira Mobile X-Ray System - Detector Not Recognized Or Image Acquisition Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for detector recognition, wireless detector communication, exposure workflow, and image acquisition failures on a Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira mobile X-ray system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot detector recognition or image acquisition problems during an active patient exam unless it is safe to stop. Remove the MOBILETT Mira from patient use if the detector is not recognized, exposures cannot be acquired, or images are not reliably appearing. Use another mobile X-ray unit if imaging is clinically urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed or dependent on unreliable imaging equipment. Why it matters: A detector or acquisition failure can cause repeat exams, delayed diagnosis, or unnecessary radiation exposure if troubleshooting is attempted during patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is detector recognition, exposure acquisition, image display, or image transfer. Ask the technologist what happened: Detector not listed or not detected; Detector connected but not ready; Exposure button works but no image appears; Image appears but is blank or corrupted; Image acquired but does not save or send; Error appears only with one detector. Check the displayed system message, detector status icon, battery indicator, and acquisition screen behavior. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before swapping parts or escalating.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the detector belongs with the MOBILETT Mira system or is approved/configured for that unit. Check the detector ID, label, or department assignment. Confirm staff did not accidentally use a detector from another room, another mobile unit, or a different imaging system. Expected outcome: The system is being tested with the correct detector. If the correct detector is recognized, stop troubleshooting and document the mismatch.

steps.title: Check Detector Battery Charge
steps.instructions: A low or depleted detector battery can prevent recognition or image acquisition. Check the detector battery indicator, charging cradle, or system status display. Install a known-charged detector battery if applicable. Allow the detector to charge if the battery is low. Expected outcome: The detector powers on and becomes available to the MOBILETT Mira. If the issue resolves after charging or battery replacement, document the low battery condition.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Detector Safely
steps.instructions: A detector may fail to reconnect after sleep mode, battery change, transport, or wireless interruption. Power off the detector if it has a power switch. Wait at least 30 seconds. Power it back on and allow time for the system to rediscover it. If the detector uses a removable battery, reseat the battery while following local handling procedures. Expected outcome: The detector reconnects and shows ready status.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check for obvious signs of damage before continuing. Inspect the detector housing, corners, connector areas, labels, battery compartment, and contact surfaces. Look for cracks, fluid intrusion, bent contacts, loose covers, missing labels, or evidence of a drop. Expected outcome: The detector appears physically intact and safe to continue testing. If physical damage is found, remove the detector from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Detector Placement and Wireless Communication Conditions
steps.instructions: Wireless detector communication can be affected by distance, shielding, storage position, or interference. Place the detector near the MOBILETT Mira. Avoid placing the detector behind lead shielding, inside storage compartments, under metal surfaces, or far from the system during testing. Confirm the unit is not in an area with unusual wireless restrictions or known network interference. Expected outcome: The detector is recognized when positioned close to the system. If recognition improves only at close range, document possible wireless communication weakness and escalate for further evaluation.

steps.title: Check Detector Docking, Charging, or Tether Connections
steps.instructions: If the system uses a docking slot, charging bay, or detector cable, check those external connection points. Inspect the docking contacts, charging contacts, detector cable, and connector ends. Look for debris, bent pins, loose connectors, damaged cable jackets, or contamination. Reseat the detector or cable firmly. Try a known-good detector cable if one is available. Expected outcome: The detector communicates normally after reseating or replacing an external connection. If the issue resolves with a cable swap, replace the faulty cable and document it.

steps.title: Restart the MOBILETT Mira System
steps.instructions: A workstation or acquisition software issue may prevent detector recognition. End any active exam if safe. Perform a normal shutdown. Wait for the system to fully power off. Restart the MOBILETT Mira and log back into the imaging workflow. Check whether the detector appears as ready. Expected outcome: The system reloads the acquisition software and recognizes the detector. If restart resolves the issue, monitor for recurrence and document the software recovery.

steps.title: Confirm Exam and Acquisition Workflow Settings
steps.instructions: The system may not acquire an image if the wrong exam, detector, body part, or acquisition mode is selected. Start a test workflow using an appropriate non-patient test process per site policy. Confirm the correct detector, exam type, receptor selection, and exposure mode are selected. Check that the system is not waiting for confirmation, patient selection, detector selection, or positioning input. Expected outcome: The detector status changes to ready and the system allows acquisition. If the issue was workflow selection, document the incorrect selection and return the unit to service after successful test acquisition.

steps.title: Check Exposure Inhibit or Readiness Messages
steps.instructions: Image acquisition may fail because the system is not actually ready to expose. Check for messages related to: Detector not ready; Battery low; Exposure inhibited; Generator not ready; Door, brake, or positioning interlock; System not prepared; Communication error; Workstation or acquisition error. Expected outcome: Any blocking condition is identified before assuming detector failure. If a non-detector interlock is causing the issue, troubleshoot that issue separately.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Detector if Available
steps.instructions: This helps separate a detector problem from a system-side problem. Use another compatible, properly assigned detector if available. Confirm the test detector is charged and approved for the MOBILETT Mira. Attempt recognition and a test acquisition according to site policy. Expected outcome: If the known-good detector works, the original detector is likely faulty. If neither detector works, the issue is likely system-side communication, software, or acquisition hardware.

steps.title: Test the Suspect Detector on Another Compatible System if Allowed
steps.instructions: Only perform this if your site allows detector cross-testing and the detector is properly configured for the other system. Test whether the suspect detector is recognized by another compatible Siemens system. Do not force pairing, reconfiguration, or service-level changes without proper authorization. Expected outcome: If the detector fails on multiple systems, suspect detector failure. If the detector works elsewhere, suspect the MOBILETT Mira system, pairing, wireless path, or acquisition workstation.

steps.title: Check Image Appearance After Acquisition
steps.instructions: If the detector is recognized but the image acquisition fails or produces unusable images, separate acquisition from image quality. Perform a controlled test image according to site policy. Check whether the image is: Missing; Delayed; Blank; Severely artifacted; Not associated with the exam; Present locally but not saved or transferred. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to acquisition, detector readout, workstation processing, or image routing. If the image is acquired locally but does not send, troubleshoot PACS/network/worklist separately.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Detector recognition failures often appear after a battery change, detector swap, software restart, network change, or physical damage event. Ask whether there were recent: Detector drops; Battery replacements; Software updates; Network changes; Department moves; New detector assignments; Previous intermittent failures; Cleaning or fluid exposure events. Expected outcome: A likely trigger is identified and documented for repair follow-up.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that the Siemens MOBILETT Mira would not recognize the wireless detector and could not acquire patient images.
CCR.Cause: Detector battery and external connections were checked; detector remained unavailable after restart and was not recognized during controlled testing.
CCR.Resolution: MOBILETT Mira and detector were removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected detector communication or acquisition system fault.

helpfulDetails: Whether the detector appeared in the acquisition software
Whether the suspect detector worked on another system
Any recent drops or fluid exposure
Whether exposure was inhibited or image acquisition completed

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira Mobile X-Ray System - Exposure inhibited due to generator, tube, or safety interlock issue
description: Exposure inhibit conditions caused by safety interlocks, detector readiness, generator status, tube limits, system position, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot exposure problems while the system is positioned over an active patient unless the exam can be safely paused. Action: Move the patient to another imaging system if imaging is urgent. Keep the MOBILETT Mira out of clinical use until exposure readiness is confirmed. Verify that no one is relying on the unit for immediate imaging. Expected outcome: Patient care continues using a known-ready imaging device. Why it matters: Exposure inhibit faults may prevent image acquisition or indicate a safety condition that should not be bypassed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Exposure Inhibit Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the user experienced before assuming generator or tube failure. Action: Power the system on and observe the startup sequence. Attempt a safe test workflow without exposing a patient. Note whether exposure is inhibited during prep, rotor/prep, exposure command, detector readiness, or after positioning. Expected outcome: The failure point is identified and documented. If the issue does not repeat: Document the reported condition, perform basic operational checks, and return to service only if the system passes normal readiness checks.

steps.title: Check for Displayed Error Messages or Status Prompts
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited because the system is waiting for a required condition. Action: Read all on-screen prompts, warnings, interlock messages, generator warnings, detector status messages, and tube heat indicators. Record the exact wording, code, or symbol shown. Expected outcome: The inhibit condition is linked to a specific system message. If a clear user-correctable prompt is shown: Correct that condition and retest. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Confirm Emergency Stop and Safety Switch Status
steps.instructions: A safety switch or emergency stop condition can prevent exposure. Action: Inspect the emergency stop button, drive handle controls, exposure hand switch, and any visible safety interlock controls. Confirm the emergency stop is not pressed or latched. Reset only according to normal external operation. Expected outcome: The system is not being held in a safety-stop condition. If the unit resumes normal exposure readiness: Document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Verify System Position and Mechanical Readiness
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray systems may inhibit exposure if the arm, tube head, column, or system position is not accepted. Action: Confirm the tube head, arm, column, and detector are positioned normally for the selected exam. Check for mechanical binding, incomplete locking, unexpected movement, or position warnings. Reposition the system and allow the console to update status. Expected outcome: The unit recognizes a valid imaging position. If repositioning clears the inhibit: Document the positioning condition and stop.

steps.title: Check Exposure Hand Switch and Connection
steps.instructions: A damaged or poorly connected exposure switch can prevent prep or exposure. Action: Inspect the exposure hand switch for damage, sticking, fluid intrusion, or cable strain. Confirm the connector is fully seated if externally accessible. Try normal prep and exposure command operation in a safe test setup. Expected outcome: The system detects prep and exposure commands correctly. If the hand switch is damaged or unreliable: Remove the unit from service for repair or accessory replacement.

steps.title: Confirm Detector Readiness
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the detector is not ready, not paired, not charged, or not communicating. Action: Check detector battery level, wireless connection, detector selection, and detector status on the workstation. Confirm the correct detector is selected for the exam. Reseat or reconnect the detector if using a docked or tethered configuration. Restart the detector if allowed by normal clinical workflow. Expected outcome: Detector status changes to ready. If detector readiness clears the exposure inhibit: Document the detector condition and stop.

steps.title: Verify Exam, Patient, and Workstation Setup
steps.instructions: The system may block exposure if the exam workflow is incomplete. Action: Confirm the correct patient, exam, body part, technique, and detector are selected. Check whether the system is waiting for exam confirmation, image receptor selection, or technique entry. Clear any incomplete or incorrect exam setup. Expected outcome: The system reaches exposure-ready status. If correcting the exam setup resolves the issue: Document the workflow issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Battery and AC Power Status
steps.instructions: Generator operation may be inhibited if the system battery is too low or power status is unstable. Action: Check battery charge level, charging status, and AC connection. Plug the system into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Verify the outlet has power and that the charging indicator behaves normally. Allow the system to charge if the battery is low. Expected outcome: Power status is stable enough for generator operation. If charging or power correction resolves the inhibit: Document the power condition and stop.

steps.title: Check Tube Heat or Generator Cooldown Status
steps.instructions: Exposure may be temporarily inhibited after repeated imaging due to tube heat or generator protection. Action: Check for tube heat, cooldown, or generator temperature warnings. Ask clinical staff whether multiple exposures were attempted rapidly. Allow the unit to cool if the system indicates a thermal limitation. Expected outcome: Exposure readiness returns after the system cools. If cooldown restores exposure: Document the thermal condition and educate users to allow recovery time during heavy use.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Cable, Connector, or Accessory Damage
steps.instructions: External damage can cause interlock, generator, detector, or exposure command problems. Action: Inspect visible cables, detector contacts, charging contacts, hand switch cable, workstation connections, and power cord. Look for crushed cables, bent pins, cracked housings, loose connectors, or signs of impact damage. Expected outcome: No obvious external damage is found. If damage is found: Remove the unit from service and send for repair.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System Safely
steps.instructions: A software or status lockup may cause a false exposure inhibit. Action: End the exam safely. Shut the MOBILETT Mira down normally. Wait briefly, then restart the system. Confirm whether the inhibit returns during startup or exam setup. Expected outcome: Temporary software state clears and exposure readiness returns. If the issue clears and does not return: Perform a functional check and document the power-cycle resolution.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Operational Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the system to service, confirm the issue is actually resolved. Action: In a controlled non-patient setting, confirm normal startup, detector readiness, exam selection, prep command, exposure readiness, and image acquisition if permitted by facility procedure. Expected outcome: The system completes a normal exposure workflow without inhibit messages. If the inhibit returns: Remove the unit from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens MOBILETT Mira would not allow exposure and displayed an exposure inhibit condition during a portable exam.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the detector was not in ready status, preventing the system from enabling exposure.
CCR.Resolution: Detector connection and exam setup were corrected, exposure readiness returned, and the system passed a non-patient operational check.

helpfulDetails: Exact inhibit message, code, or icon displayed
