Whether inhibit occurs during prep, exposure, startup, or exam setup
Battery level and AC charging status
Any unusual sounds, heat, smell, or impact damage
Whether power cycling changed the behavior

title: Siemens Healthineers MOBILETT Mira Mobile X-Ray System - Drive, brake, column, or arm positioning fault
description: Drive, brake, column, or arm positioning issues that prevent safe movement, positioning, parking, or bedside imaging.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot movement or arm positioning issues while the unit is close to a patient, bed, stretcher, staff member, or wall. Action: Move the patient to another imaging option if imaging is urgent. Keep the MOBILETT Mira away from the patient until the movement or positioning fault is understood. If the unit cannot be moved safely, stop and request help before attempting repositioning. Expected outcome: Patient and staff safety are protected before mechanical or drive troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: Mobile X-ray systems are heavy, powered, and have moving arms, brakes, and columns. A positioning fault can create collision, crush, or tip hazards.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm what clinical staff actually experienced. Action: Ask whether the fault involved: Drive assist not working. Brake stuck or not holding. Column not raising or lowering. Arm not extending, rotating, or locking properly. System refusing exposure due to positioning. Unusual resistance, drifting, grinding, or clicking. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to drive, brake, column, arm, or positioning behavior. Why it matters: “Positioning fault” can mean either a mechanical movement issue or a system interlock preventing exposure.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the unit externally before assuming a motor, brake, or column failure. Action: Check around the wheels, base, column, arm, tube head, handle, and detector storage area. Look for: Cords wrapped around wheels. Debris under casters. Bed linens caught in the base. Accessory bags blocking movement. Objects preventing arm travel. Tube head or arm contacting nearby equipment. Expected outcome: Any external obstruction is removed and movement returns to normal. If this resolves the issue, return the unit to service after confirming safe movement and documenting the obstruction.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Fully Powered On and Ready
steps.instructions: Drive and positioning functions may not behave correctly if the system is not fully initialized. Action: Check that the system is powered on normally. Confirm the display is responsive. Look for active warning messages, interlock messages, or startup errors. Allow the unit to complete boot-up before testing movement. Expected outcome: The system reaches normal operating status without startup or safety warnings. Why it matters: Some movement, brake, or exposure functions may be inhibited until the system completes startup checks.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Status
steps.instructions: Low battery condition can affect drive assist, motorized movement, brakes, or system readiness. Action: Check the battery indicator or system battery status. Connect the unit to a known-good AC outlet if battery charge is low. Allow adequate charge time if the battery is depleted. Observe whether movement or positioning functions improve while connected to AC power. Expected outcome: The unit has enough battery capacity to support powered movement and positioning. If the fault clears after charging, document the battery condition and verify the unit operates normally before returning it to service.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Charging Connection
steps.instructions: A poor charging connection can lead to low battery complaints that appear as drive or positioning problems. Action: Inspect the power cord for damage, looseness, bent blades, or strain. Verify the cord is seated correctly at the unit and wall outlet. Test the outlet if there is concern about power. Check for charging indicators on the unit. Expected outcome: The unit charges normally and does not show power or battery-related warnings. If charging is abnormal, remove the unit from service for electrical safety and charging system evaluation.

steps.title: Check Brake Release and Brake Engagement Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is a stuck brake, brake not holding, or normal brake engagement being mistaken for a fault. Action: On a clear, level floor, test whether the unit releases and rolls as expected. Check whether the brake engages when the handle or controls are released. Listen for abnormal brake clicking, dragging, or grinding. Verify the unit does not roll unexpectedly when parked. Expected outcome: Brakes release when commanded and hold the unit securely when parked. If the brake does not hold, do not return the unit to service. Remove it from use because uncontrolled movement is a safety hazard.

steps.title: Check Drive Handle or Drive Assist Response
steps.instructions: If the unit has powered drive assist, confirm whether the handle input is being recognized. Action: With the path clear, gently test forward and reverse movement. Confirm whether the drive assist responds smoothly. Check whether the issue occurs in one direction only or both directions. Observe for delay, jerking, sudden stopping, or no response. Expected outcome: Drive assist responds predictably and stops safely when released. If the drive response is erratic, remove the unit from service. Do not continue clinical use with unpredictable powered movement.

steps.title: Inspect Wheels and Casters
steps.instructions: Wheel or caster problems can mimic drive motor, brake, or steering faults. Action: Check all visible wheels and casters for: Flat spots. Loose debris. Fluid residue. Broken tread. Objects lodged in the wheel. Uneven rolling. Resistance during manual movement. Expected outcome: Wheels rotate freely and the unit tracks normally. If a wheel or caster is physically damaged, remove the unit from service for repair.

steps.title: Check Floor and Environment Conditions
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray drive problems may be caused by the environment rather than the unit. Action: Test movement on a clear, dry, level floor. Avoid thresholds, elevator gaps, wet areas, floor mats, cords, and uneven transitions. Compare behavior in the reported area versus a controlled open area. Expected outcome: The issue either follows the unit or is linked to the floor/environment. If the unit operates normally in a clear area, document the environmental cause and educate staff as appropriate.

steps.title: Verify Column Movement
steps.instructions: Check whether the column raises, lowers, rotates, or locks normally, depending on the reported issue. Action: In a safe open area, move the column through normal external positioning ranges without forcing it. Watch for: Binding. Uneven movement. Failure to lock. Drifting after release. Unexpected stopping. Warning messages. Expected outcome: Column movement is smooth, controlled, and secure after positioning. If the column does not lock or drifts, remove the unit from service. A drifting column or tube arm is a patient and staff safety risk.

steps.title: Verify Arm and Tube Head Positioning
steps.instructions: Inspect the arm and tube head externally for safe positioning behavior. Action: Move the arm and tube head through normal positioning motions. Confirm locks, detents, and release controls behave normally. Check whether the arm holds position without dropping, drifting, or springing back. Do not force the arm past resistance. Expected outcome: The arm and tube head move smoothly and remain stable when released. If the arm drops, drifts, sticks, or will not lock, remove the unit from service for mechanical evaluation.

steps.title: Check Parking and Transport Position
steps.instructions: Some mobile X-ray systems may restrict drive, exposure, or movement if the column, arm, detector, or tube head is not properly parked or positioned. Action: Return the arm, tube head, and detector storage components to their normal transport or park position. Check for any positioning prompts or interlock messages. Verify whether the fault clears after proper parking. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the unit position and allows normal transport or operation. If the fault clears, confirm proper movement and document the positioning/interlock condition.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Mechanical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for signs the unit was hit, dropped, forced, or driven into an object. Action: Inspect the base, column, arm, tube head, handle, bumpers, and covers. Look for: Cracked covers. Bent arm components. Loose panels. Scrape marks. Misalignment. Unusual gaps. Hardware visibly out of place. Expected outcome: No obvious damage is found, or damage is identified as the likely cause. If damage is present, remove the unit from service and route it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Restart the System if Safe
steps.instructions: A software or control state issue may clear after a controlled restart. Action: Move the unit to a safe location. Power down the system using the normal shutdown method. Wait briefly, then restart the unit. Observe whether the same drive, brake, column, or arm fault returns. Expected outcome: The unit restarts normally and movement/positioning functions operate correctly. If the fault does not return, perform a careful functional check before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the unit to clinical use, verify the problem is resolved. Action: Confirm: Unit powers on normally. Battery status is acceptable. Brakes release and hold correctly. Drive assist responds smoothly. Wheels roll freely. Column and arm move safely. Tube head holds position. No active warnings remain. Expected outcome: The unit is safe to move, position, and use for bedside imaging. If any movement, brake, column, or arm behavior remains abnormal, do not return the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens MOBILETT Mira was difficult to move and displayed a positioning-related fault when attempting bedside imaging.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the tube arm was not fully returned to its transport position, causing the system to remain in a safety interlock condition.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned the arm into the proper park position, confirmed the warning cleared, verified brake release, drive assist, column movement, and arm stability, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested, if charging or battery condition was involved
Battery level at time of inspection
Whether the issue occurred while driving, parking, positioning, or exposing
Brake behavior: stuck, dragging, not holding, or normal
Drive behavior: no assist, jerking, one-direction fault, or intermittent
Arm/tube head lock and drift behavior
Evidence of impact or physical damage

title: Siemens Healthineers Ysio Max Radiographic System - Wireless Detector Not Recognized or Losing Connection
description: Wireless detector recognition, intermittent detector connection, acquisition readiness issues, or lost detector communication on a Siemens Ysio Max radiographic system.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot detector communication problems during an active patient exposure setup. Action: Remove the patient from the table or wall stand area if imaging cannot continue safely. Notify clinical staff that the detector is not reliably connected. Use another room, detector, or imaging system if patient care is time-sensitive. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on an unstable detector connection. Why it matters: A wireless detector that drops connection can delay imaging, cause repeat positioning, or interrupt acquisition workflow.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the detector is completely unrecognized or intermittently disconnecting. Action: Ask staff when the issue occurs: At system startup. When selecting the detector. When moving the detector. During exam setup. At exposure attempt. After image acquisition. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified before replacing hardware or escalating. Why it matters: A detector that never appears may have a power, battery, registration, or network issue. A detector that drops out during movement may suggest battery, wireless signal, docking, or detector damage.

steps.title: 3. Check Detector Power Status
steps.instructions: Inspect the wireless detector for power indication. Action: Confirm the detector is powered on. Check detector indicator lights, display, or status LEDs if present. Power cycle the detector if allowed by site procedure. Expected outcome: The detector powers on normally and becomes available to the system. If this resolves the issue, return the detector to service after confirming normal recognition and test image acquisition.

steps.title: 4. Check Detector Battery Charge
steps.instructions: A low or weak detector battery can cause recognition failure or intermittent wireless dropouts. Action: Remove the detector battery if applicable. Inspect for obvious damage, swelling, poor seating, contamination, or loose fit. Install a fully charged known-good battery. Place the original battery on the charger and confirm charging indication. Expected outcome: The detector remains connected with a known-good charged battery. If the detector works normally with another battery, document the failed or weak battery and remove it from service.

steps.title: 5. Verify Detector Docking or Charging Station Function
steps.instructions: If the detector uses a charging dock or storage slot, confirm it is charging and communicating normally. Action: Place the detector in the dock. Check for charging status or system recognition. Inspect the dock contacts for debris, bent pins, damage, or fluid contamination. Confirm the dock has power. Expected outcome: The detector charges normally and is detected when docked. If the dock does not charge or recognize any detector, the dock or associated connection may require repair evaluation.

steps.title: 6. Confirm the Correct Detector Is Selected
steps.instructions: The system may not acquire images if the wrong detector, room receptor, or acquisition mode is selected. Action: Check the selected detector/receptor on the acquisition workstation. Confirm the exam is set up for the wireless detector being used. Verify staff are not trying to use a detector assigned to another room or system. Expected outcome: The correct wireless detector is selected and ready for acquisition. If this resolves the issue, no repair is needed unless the incorrect selection keeps recurring due to configuration problems.

steps.title: 7. Check Detector Seating and Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Wireless detectors are frequently handled, moved, and placed under patients, which increases the chance of damage. Action: Inspect the detector exterior for cracks, impact damage, loose covers, damaged handles, or signs of fluid intrusion. Gently check whether the battery latch, detector cover, or connector areas appear secure. Do not open the detector housing. Expected outcome: No obvious physical damage is found. If damage is found, remove the detector from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 8. Check Wireless Signal Conditions
steps.instructions: Wireless detector communication may drop if the detector is too far from the system, shielded, or blocked. Action: Test the detector near the table or wall stand in its normal clinical position. Avoid placing the detector behind thick barriers, metal carts, equipment, or other signal-blocking objects. Ask staff whether the issue happens only in certain detector positions or only near certain equipment. Expected outcome: The detector maintains connection in normal use positions. If connection only fails in specific locations, document the location and possible wireless interference pattern.

steps.title: 9. Restart the Acquisition Workstation and Imaging System
steps.instructions: Software or communication services may fail to refresh detector status. Action: Follow site-approved shutdown and restart procedure for the workstation/system. Allow the system to fully boot. Power cycle the wireless detector after the system is ready. Check whether the detector appears available. Expected outcome: Detector communication is restored after a clean restart. If this resolves the issue, perform a basic functional check before returning the room to use.

steps.title: 10. Test With Another Known-Good Detector if Available
steps.instructions: This helps separate a detector problem from a room/system communication problem. Action: Connect or select another compatible known-good wireless detector. Check whether the second detector is recognized and remains connected. Then test the suspect detector again if safe and appropriate. Expected outcome: If the known-good detector works, the original detector or battery is likely the issue. If no detectors are recognized, the issue may be with the room system, workstation, wireless communication hardware, or configuration.

steps.title: 11. Check Network or Room Communication Indicators
steps.instructions: A detector may rely on room communication, workstation services, wireless access points, or system configuration. Action: Check for room network alerts, workstation communication errors, or detector status warnings. Confirm the acquisition workstation is otherwise functioning normally. Verify that other room functions are not also showing communication errors. Expected outcome: No broader system communication issue is present. If multiple communication-related problems are present, escalate the room/system for service evaluation.

steps.title: 12. Perform a Controlled Functional Test
steps.instructions: After any corrective action, confirm the detector is stable before returning the room to service. Action: Select the wireless detector. Verify ready status. Perform a non-patient test exposure or site-approved detector check if allowed. Confirm image acquisition and transfer. Move the detector through normal use positions and watch for connection drops. Expected outcome: The detector remains recognized and image acquisition completes normally. If the detector disconnects again, remove it from service or escalate the room depending on test results.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Ysio Max wireless detector was not recognized and intermittently lost connection during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the detector battery was low and the detector connection dropped when moved away from the charging area.
CCR.Resolution: Installed a fully charged known-good battery, verified detector recognition, completed test acquisition, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurs docked or wireless only
Any drops, fluid exposure, cracks, or visible damage
Room location where connection was lost

title: Siemens Healthineers Ysio Max Radiographic System - System Startup Failure or Acquisition Workstation Freeze
description: Startup failure, frozen acquisition workstation, boot delays, display lockups, or system readiness issues on a Siemens Ysio Max radiographic system.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the Ysio Max during an active patient exam if the system is frozen, partially booted, or not responding. Action: Stop the exam workflow. Move the patient away from the table or wall stand if positioning or exposure status is uncertain. Use another radiographic room or portable X-ray system if imaging is clinically urgent. Do not attempt exposures from a system that has not completed startup or workstation readiness. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on an unstable imaging system. Why it matters: A frozen workstation or incomplete startup can prevent image review, exposure control, safety interlock confirmation, or proper exam documentation.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is with the entire radiographic system or only the acquisition workstation. Action: Check if the system powers on at all. Note whether the generator, table, wall stand, tube stand, or workstation display responds. Confirm whether the freeze occurs: During initial boot. At user login. When opening the acquisition software. When selecting a patient. After exposure. During image processing or transfer. Record any displayed error messages or frozen screen text. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to system power/startup, acquisition computer/software, or workflow-related lockup. Why it matters: “System will not start” and “workstation frozen” can have different causes and different escalation paths.

steps.title: 3. Check Room Power and Emergency-Off Status
steps.instructions: Verify that the room has proper power and that no emergency stop or room shutdown condition is active. Action: Confirm the room power indicator is normal, if present. Check the main wall power switch or room control power switch. Verify that any emergency-off button is not pressed. Inspect nearby breaker panels only if they are accessible and permitted by facility policy. Ask staff whether there was a recent power outage, shutdown, or room reset. Expected outcome: The system has normal facility power and is not disabled by an external emergency-off condition. If the system starts normally after restoring room power or releasing an emergency-off condition, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Workstation Power and Display
steps.instructions: Check whether the acquisition workstation itself is powered and displaying correctly. Action: Verify the workstation monitor is powered on. Check for power indicator lights on the workstation computer. Confirm the display cable is secure at the monitor and computer. If there are multiple monitors, verify whether all displays are frozen or only one is blank. Check the keyboard and mouse connections. Expected outcome: The workstation has power, video output, and input devices connected. Why it matters: A blank or frozen display may be mistaken for a full system failure when the issue is limited to the monitor, workstation, or input device.

steps.title: 5. Look for Simple Peripheral or Accessory Problems
steps.instructions: Check for external devices that may interfere with startup or workstation operation. Action: Inspect USB devices, barcode scanners, keyboard, mouse, and any external media devices. Remove nonessential USB drives or accessories if allowed by site policy. Check for damaged cables, loose connectors, or recently added peripherals. Confirm that no keyboard key or mouse button appears stuck. Expected outcome: External peripherals are ruled out as a cause of boot delay, input lockup, or workstation freeze. If removing a nonessential external device allows normal startup, document the accessory issue and stop.

steps.title: 6. Perform a Controlled System Restart
steps.instructions: If the system is frozen and no patient is connected or actively being imaged, perform a controlled restart according to site policy. Action: Use the normal shutdown or restart process if the workstation responds. If the workstation is fully locked, follow the facility-approved power cycle process. Allow the system enough time to shut down and restart completely. Do not repeatedly power cycle the system in rapid succession. Observe the startup sequence and note where the system stops or freezes. Expected outcome: The Ysio Max restarts, acquisition software loads, and the system reaches ready status. If the restart clears the issue and the system passes normal readiness checks, return it to service after confirming proper operation.

steps.title: 7. Confirm Login and Network Access
steps.instructions: If the workstation boots but freezes or fails during login or patient/worklist access, check user and network-related causes. Action: Confirm whether the issue occurs with one user account or all users. Check for a network connection indicator if available. Verify the Ethernet cable is connected and undamaged. Ask staff whether PACS, RIS, worklist, or hospital network issues are affecting other imaging rooms. Test whether local functions respond even if worklist access does not. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into workstation freeze, login problem, or network/worklist dependency. Why it matters: A worklist or network failure can make the workstation appear unusable even when the X-ray system itself is operational.

steps.title: 8. Check for Acquisition Software Freeze
steps.instructions: If the operating system responds but the acquisition application is locked, focus on the software state. Action: Note whether the mouse and keyboard still respond. Check whether only the acquisition software is frozen. Look for pending dialog boxes, error popups, or windows hidden behind the main application. Restart the acquisition application only if permitted by local procedure. If the software reopens, verify patient selection, detector status, and image acquisition readiness. Expected outcome: The acquisition software either recovers normally or the fault is confirmed as a recurring workstation/software issue. If the application freezes again during the same workflow, remove the room from service and escalate.

steps.title: 9. Verify Detector and System Communication Status
steps.instructions: If the workstation freezes during detector selection, image acquisition, or image processing, check external communication points. Action: Confirm the detector is powered or docked as expected. Check detector battery status if applicable. Verify detector docking contacts or connection points are clean and undamaged. Confirm the selected detector matches the exam room setup. Check whether the freeze happens only with one detector or all detectors. Expected outcome: Detector-related communication problems are identified or ruled out. Why it matters: Acquisition workstations may hang or fail to proceed if they cannot communicate properly with the selected detector or imaging chain.

steps.title: 10. Check for Environmental or Overheating Concerns
steps.instructions: Inspect the workstation and equipment area for obvious environmental problems. Action: Check that workstation vents are not blocked. Listen for unusual fan noise from the workstation or system cabinet. Look for unusual heat, burning smell, or visible dust buildup around vents. Confirm the room is not excessively hot. Do not continue operating equipment that smells hot or appears overheated. Expected outcome: Obvious overheating or ventilation concerns are identified before continued use. If overheating, smoke, odor, or abnormal noise is present, remove the system from service immediately.

steps.title: 11. Test Basic System Readiness After Recovery
steps.instructions: If the system starts successfully after troubleshooting, verify basic readiness before returning it to clinical use. Action: Confirm the acquisition workstation is responsive. Confirm the system reaches normal ready status. Verify detector recognition. Confirm table, wall stand, and tube stand status indicators appear normal. Confirm no startup errors remain. Perform any required site-approved operational check before releasing the room. Expected outcome: The Ysio Max is stable, responsive, and ready for clinical imaging. If the system freezes again, does not complete startup, or produces recurring errors, do not return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens Ysio Max acquisition workstation froze during startup and the room could not be used for patient imaging.
CCR.Cause: Verified the workstation powered on but repeatedly locked before the acquisition software reached ready status; room power, emergency-off status, monitor connections, keyboard, mouse, and external cabling were checked with no external fault found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Ysio Max room from service, labeled the system Out of Service, documented the startup freeze behavior, and escalated for workstation/software evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the full system failed or only the acquisition workstation froze
Exact screen, message, or boot stage where the freeze occurred
Whether the issue happened before or after user login
Whether the monitor had power and signal
Alarm behavior or displayed startup messages
Accessories or peripherals removed, swapped, or tested
Power behavior during startup, shutdown, or restart
Environmental factors such as heat, blocked vents, dust, odor, or fan noise
Indicator lights on the workstation, monitor, system cabinet, detector, or room power controls
Whether a controlled restart restored operation
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Siemens Healthineers Ysio Max Radiographic System - Auto-Positioning, Tube Stand, or Wall Stand Movement Fault
description: Troubleshooting Ysio Max auto-positioning, tube stand, wall stand, or motorized movement faults caused by setup, obstruction, locks, or positioning limits.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot movement faults while a patient is positioned in the system’s travel path. Remove the patient from the table, wall stand, or exam area if movement is unreliable. Keep staff clear of the tube stand, detector stand, table, and any motorized travel path. Stop all auto-positioning attempts if the system moves unexpectedly, stalls, or reports a movement fault. Expected outcome: The area is safe before troubleshooting continues. Why it matters: Motorized movement faults can create pinch, collision, or positioning hazards if troubleshooting is performed around a patient.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Movement Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly which part of the system is affected. Ask staff whether the issue involves auto-positioning not starting. Ask staff whether the issue involves the tube stand not moving. Ask staff whether the issue involves the wall stand not moving. Ask staff whether the issue involves a table tracking issue. Ask staff whether the issue involves a detector alignment issue. Ask staff whether the issue involves movement stopping mid-travel. Ask staff whether the issue involves unexpected brake or lock behavior. Ask staff whether the issue involves a collision warning or movement inhibit. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to auto-positioning, tube stand, wall stand, detector, or table-related movement. If this identifies user setup confusion or a normal safety inhibit, correct the setup and stop.

steps.title: 3. Check for Obstructions in the Travel Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the system before assuming a mechanical failure. Check around the tube stand, ceiling rails, wall stand, table, detector area, and floor space. Remove stools, step platforms, cables, linen, detector holders, positioning aids, or other equipment from the movement path. Confirm nothing is blocking wall stand vertical travel or tube stand longitudinal/transverse movement. Expected outcome: The system has a clear path for movement. If removing an obstruction restores normal movement, return the unit to service and document the finding.

steps.title: 4. Check Emergency Stop and Movement Inhibit Conditions
steps.instructions: A pressed emergency stop or active safety inhibit may prevent motion. Inspect accessible emergency stop buttons. Confirm none are pressed or latched. Reset only according to normal site procedure. Check for displayed messages indicating movement inhibit, collision protection, brake status, or safety stop. Expected outcome: No emergency stop or active safety inhibit is preventing movement. If the issue clears after releasing an emergency stop, test normal movement and document the cause.

steps.title: 5. Confirm System Power and Normal Startup Status
steps.instructions: Movement faults can occur if part of the system did not initialize correctly. Confirm the radiographic system is fully powered on. Verify the operator console, generator, tube stand, wall stand, and table appear ready. Look for warning messages, initialization errors, or communication faults. Perform a normal restart if allowed by site workflow and the room is not in clinical use. Expected outcome: The system completes startup without active motion or communication faults. If restart restores movement and no fault returns, perform a basic functional check before returning to service.

steps.title: 6. Check Tube Stand and Wall Stand Locks or Brakes
steps.instructions: Mechanical locks, brake release issues, or manual positioning controls can cause movement complaints. Verify the tube stand is not locked in a position that prevents requested travel. Check whether the wall stand is locked, parked, or at a physical travel limit. Operate normal brake release or movement controls as designed. Listen for brake release sounds and observe whether the stand attempts to move. Expected outcome: Brakes release normally and the stand moves smoothly within its allowed range. If a lock or brake selection was the cause, correct it, retest, and stop.

steps.title: 7. Confirm the Requested Position Is Valid
steps.instructions: Auto-positioning may fail if the selected position is outside the system’s safe or allowed travel range. Check the selected exam program, SID, detector location, table position, wall stand position, and tube angle. Confirm the system is not already at a hard travel limit. Move the tube stand or wall stand slightly away from end-of-travel if safe to do so. Try a simple manual movement before retrying auto-positioning. Expected outcome: The system accepts a valid position and moves normally. Why it matters: Auto-positioning depends on the system knowing a reachable target position.

steps.title: 8. Check Detector and Bucky Positioning
steps.instructions: Auto-positioning may be inhibited if the detector, bucky, or wall stand position is not recognized. Confirm the detector is properly seated in the table bucky or wall stand bucky if the exam requires it. Check that the bucky tray is fully inserted and latched. Verify the selected receptor matches the intended exam location. Confirm no accessory or grid is preventing full seating. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the detector/receptor position and permits movement. If reseating the detector or bucky resolves the fault, document the positioning issue and return the system to service.

steps.title: 9. Inspect External Cables and Accessible Connections
steps.instructions: Loose or stressed cables can affect communication with motorized components. Inspect visible cables around the tube stand, wall stand, table, and operator console. Look for unplugged connectors, damaged insulation, pulled cable chains, pinched cables, or loose strain reliefs. Do not open covers or perform internal cable repair during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No obvious external cable damage or disconnection is found. If damage is found, remove the system from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: 10. Test Manual Movement Separately
steps.instructions: Separate auto-positioning faults from basic motorized movement faults. Attempt safe manual movement of the affected component using normal controls. Test only one axis or component at a time, such as tube stand longitudinal movement. Test only one axis or component at a time, such as tube stand vertical movement. Test only one axis or component at a time, such as tube angulation. Test only one axis or component at a time, such as wall stand vertical movement. Test only one axis or component at a time, such as table movement, if related. Observe whether movement is smooth, delayed, noisy, jerky, or completely inhibited. Expected outcome: Manual movement helps identify whether the issue is auto-positioning logic or a movement hardware/sensing problem. If manual movement works but auto-positioning fails, document that distinction clearly.

steps.title: 11. Check for Collision or Alignment Warnings
steps.instructions: Radiographic rooms use safety logic to prevent unsafe positioning. Review the system display for collision warnings, alignment prompts, tracking errors, or positioning messages. Check whether the tube, wall stand, table, or detector is too close to another component. Move the system to a neutral safe position if possible. Retry movement only after the warning clears. Expected outcome: Collision protection clears once the system is safely repositioned. If the warning remains with no obstruction present, escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: 12. Perform a Limited Functional Check
steps.instructions: After any correction, verify safe basic operation before returning the room to use. Confirm the affected component moves through a small safe range. Confirm braking and stopping behavior appears normal. Confirm auto-positioning works for a simple known exam position, if appropriate. Check that no warning or fault returns. Expected outcome: Movement is smooth, predictable, and free of repeated faults. If the system passes functional checks, return it to service and document the work order.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the Siemens Ysio Max would not auto-position and the wall stand stopped during vertical movement.
CCR.Cause: Found the wall stand travel path partially blocked by a positioning step stool, causing the system to inhibit movement.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the obstruction, verified wall stand and tube stand movement, confirmed auto-positioning completed normally, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or exact movement fault/message displayed
Accessories swapped, detector reseated, or bucky reseated
Power behavior, outlet or room power status, and whether system startup completed normally
Environmental factors, obstructions removed, emergency stop status, travel path condition, or room setup concerns
Indicator lights, brake or lock behavior, and whether manual movement worked
Final device status, including whether the room was returned to service or removed from service

title: Siemens Healthineers Ysio Max Radiographic System - Exposure Blocked By Generator, Tube, Or Door/Interlock Condition
description: Exposure blocked when the Ysio Max detects generator readiness, tube, room door, or system interlock conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot exposure-blocking faults while the patient is positioned for an active exam unless the issue can be resolved safely and immediately. Action: Stop exposure attempts. Inform the technologist that the system is not ready for exposure. Move the patient away from the table or wall stand if troubleshooting may delay care. Use another radiographic room or portable imaging system if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without repeated failed exposure attempts or unnecessary radiation-related risk. Why it matters: Exposure interlocks are safety controls. Repeated attempts to force an exposure should never be treated as normal troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Exposure Block
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what condition is being reported before assuming a generator or tube failure. Action: Review the operator display, generator console, or system status message. Ask the technologist when the issue occurs: At exam start. During positioning. When pressing prep. When attempting exposure. Only with a specific wall stand, table, detector, or room setup. Document the exact message, warning, or indicator shown. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified and can be separated into a setup issue, interlock issue, generator issue, or tube readiness issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Exam Room Is Safe for Exposure
steps.instructions: Check external room safety conditions first. Action: Verify the radiographic room door is fully closed. Check whether any door indicator, warning light, or room interlock indicator shows open or unsafe. Confirm no emergency stop, room safety switch, or exposure inhibit condition is active. Ask staff if the issue started after door work, room maintenance, construction, or electrical work. Expected outcome: If the door or room interlock clears and exposure becomes available, the issue was external to the imaging system. Stop troubleshooting. Why it matters: A door or room interlock can intentionally block exposure even when the imaging system itself is functional.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and System Reset Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that no emergency stop or safety stop is engaged. Action: Inspect accessible emergency stop buttons in the room and at the operator area. Confirm none are pressed, latched, damaged, or partially engaged. Reset only if safe and allowed by site procedure. Power cycle the system only after confirming the patient is clear and the room is safe. Expected outcome: If the system returns to normal ready status, the exposure block was caused by a safety stop or latched inhibit condition.

steps.title: Verify Power and System Startup Status
steps.instructions: Make sure all major system components are powered and fully initialized. Action: Confirm the Ysio Max system is fully powered on. Check the generator, workstation, table, wall stand, detector, and tube stand status. Look for warning lights, fault indicators, incomplete startup messages, or communication warnings. Confirm the system is not still booting or waiting for component initialization. Expected outcome: All system components should show normal ready status before exposure is attempted. Why it matters: Generator or tube readiness may be blocked if a required subsystem has not initialized correctly.

steps.title: Confirm Tube Stand and Wall Stand Positioning
steps.instructions: Check for positioning conditions that may prevent exposure. Action: Verify the tube is properly positioned for the selected receptor. Confirm the tube, table, or wall stand is not outside a permitted exposure range. Check that the selected receptor matches the actual physical setup. Confirm the system recognizes the active imaging position. Expected outcome: If repositioning the tube or selecting the correct receptor clears the block, the issue was a positioning or configuration mismatch.

steps.title: Check Collimator and Tube Readiness
steps.instructions: Verify that the tube and collimator appear ready for exposure. Action: Inspect the collimator display or indicators if present. Confirm the tube is not reporting a heat, rotation, prep, or readiness fault. Listen for abnormal sounds during prep, such as failed rotor start, unusual humming, clicking, or silence when prep should occur. Check whether the exposure block happens during prep or only when full exposure is pressed. Expected outcome: The system should move from standby to prep and then to exposure-ready without tube or generator warnings. Why it matters: A generator or tube issue may not appear until the system attempts prep or exposure.

steps.title: Verify Technique and Exam Settings
steps.instructions: Check for settings that may create an exposure inhibit. Action: Confirm the selected exam, detector, SID, bucky, and technique are appropriate. Check that kVp, mA, mAs, AEC, and exposure mode values are not invalid or outside allowed limits. Try a basic approved test technique only if the room is safe, no patient is present, and site policy allows. Avoid repeated exposure attempts if the same block returns. Expected outcome: If correcting the technique or exam selection clears the issue, the device does not require repair.

steps.title: Check Detector and Bucky Recognition
steps.instructions: Confirm the system recognizes the selected image receptor. Action: Verify the correct detector is selected at the workstation. Confirm the detector is seated properly in the table or wall stand bucky if applicable. Check for detector battery, connection, charging, or communication warnings. Try another approved detector or receptor location if available. Expected outcome: If the exposure block clears after correcting receptor recognition, the problem was related to detector or bucky selection rather than the generator.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Cables and External Connections
steps.instructions: Check for obvious external connection issues. Action: Inspect accessible cables at the table, wall stand, operator console, detector dock, and generator area without opening covers. Look for loose connectors, damaged cable jackets, pulled strain reliefs, or recently disturbed cables. Confirm no equipment cart, table movement, or floor traffic has damaged visible cabling. Expected outcome: Any loose or damaged external connection should be corrected or escalated according to site policy.

steps.title: Review Recent Service or Room Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue appeared after a known change. Action: Ask staff if the problem started after: Preventive maintenance. Software update. Room cleaning. Door repair. Detector swap. Generator service. Tube replacement. Power outage. Check recent work orders for related faults. Expected outcome: A recent change may point toward a configuration, interlock, calibration, or service-related issue.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Restart if Appropriate
steps.instructions: Only restart the system if it is safe and does not interrupt patient care. Action: Remove the patient from the room or imaging position. Follow normal shutdown and startup procedure. Allow the system to complete full initialization. Recheck generator, tube, detector, door, and system-ready status. Expected outcome: If the exposure block clears and does not return, document the restart and verify normal operation before returning to service.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is ready before returning it to clinical use. Action: Verify no active errors remain. Confirm door and room interlock status behaves normally. Confirm the selected detector and receptor are recognized. Perform only site-approved test exposures or QA checks if permitted. Confirm images process and transfer normally if a test exposure is completed. Expected outcome: The system should complete normal prep and exposure without generator, tube, or interlock warnings.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Siemens Ysio Max would not expose and displayed an exposure blocked condition during attempted chest imaging.
CCR.Cause: Verified room door was closed, emergency stop was reset, detector was recognized, and technique settings were valid, but the system continued to block exposure during prep with generator/tube readiness warnings.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the radiographic room from service, labeled the Ysio Max Out of Service, documented the exposure inhibit behavior, and escalated for Siemens service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact exposure block message or status shown
Whether the issue occurred at prep or exposure
Detector selected and detector recognition status
Any generator, tube, rotor, heat, or interlock warning
Indicator lights or warning lamps observed
Recent service, power outage, or room maintenance

title: Siemens Healthineers Ysio Max Radiographic System - DICOM Send, Modality Worklist, or Network Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting Ysio Max DICOM send failures, missing Modality Worklist exams, or network communication issues before escalating for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot network or image transfer problems while delaying urgent patient care. Confirm whether the patient exam is complete. If imaging is still needed, have staff continue using the Ysio Max locally if acquisition is working. If the system cannot safely support the exam workflow, move the patient to another radiographic room or approved backup process. Expected outcome: Patient care continues while the communication issue is isolated. Why it matters: A DICOM or worklist issue is usually not an exposure safety issue, but it can delay care, prevent image availability, or create documentation risk. If this resolves the clinical workflow concern, document the downtime process and continue troubleshooting when safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Communication Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what part of the workflow is failing. Ask staff whether the issue is: DICOM send failure, images stuck in queue, PACS destination unavailable, Modality Worklist not updating, wrong patient data returned, unable to query worklist, network connection warning, remote viewer or archive unavailable. Check whether image acquisition itself still works. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to sending, receiving worklist data, network access, or destination availability. Why it matters: A worklist failure, PACS send failure, and total network loss are different problems even though staff may report them all as “network down.”

steps.title: Check for Hospital-Wide PACS, RIS, or Network Issues
steps.instructions: Before assuming the Ysio Max is defective, determine whether other systems are affected. Check another imaging room, mobile X-ray, CT, or workstation if available. Ask imaging staff whether PACS, RIS, or worklist issues are occurring elsewhere. Contact the help desk, PACS administrator, or radiology IT support if a broader outage is suspected. Expected outcome: You determine whether the problem is isolated to the Ysio Max or part of a larger network/PACS/RIS issue. If multiple systems are affected, stop device-level troubleshooting and document that the issue appears external to the Ysio Max.

steps.title: Confirm the System Is Fully Powered and Logged In
steps.instructions: Verify that the acquisition workstation and imaging system are in a normal ready state. Check that the Ysio Max workstation is powered on. Confirm the user is logged in properly. Look for system warnings, communication icons, queue errors, or application messages. Confirm the system date and time appear correct. Expected outcome: The system is operational and not blocked by a login, software state, or obvious workstation error. Why it matters: DICOM and worklist communication can fail if the workstation is not fully initialized, the imaging application is not running correctly, or system time is incorrect.

steps.title: Check the Physical Network Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the external network connection before changing settings. Check the network cable at the wall jack and workstation/network connection point. Look for a loose, damaged, stretched, or unplugged cable. Verify link lights if visible at the network port or network adapter. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct wall jack if the room has multiple ports. Expected outcome: The system has a secure physical network connection. If the cable was loose or disconnected, reconnect it, allow the system to re-establish communication, then retry the worklist query or DICOM send. If this resolves the issue, stop and document the correction.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Network Port or Wall Jack Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Make sure the system is not connected to an inactive or wrong network drop. Compare the connection to room labeling or known-good configuration. Ask IT or facilities whether the wall jack is active. If allowed by site policy, test the port with an approved network tester or have IT verify switch-port activity. Expected outcome: The Ysio Max is connected to an active network drop assigned for imaging equipment. Why it matters: Radiographic systems often require specific network routing, firewall rules, VLAN access, or static addressing. A normal-looking network jack may not be configured for DICOM traffic.

steps.title: Review the DICOM Send Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether images are failing, pending, or waiting to send. Open the DICOM send/export queue or image transfer status area. Look for failed jobs, pending jobs, repeated retries, or destination errors. Note the destination name, time of failure, accession number, and any displayed error text. Retry one failed image only if site policy allows and the destination is believed to be available. Expected outcome: You identify whether the system is creating images but failing during transmission. If a retry succeeds, monitor the queue until it clears, then document that the DICOM queue recovered. If failures continue, proceed with network and destination checks.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Destination Is Selected
steps.instructions: Verify that images are being sent to the intended PACS, archive, or routing destination. Check whether the selected destination matches the normal site workflow. Confirm staff did not accidentally select an alternate destination, test destination, CD/DVD export, local-only storage, or wrong archive. Compare with another working room if needed. Expected outcome: The image transfer destination is appropriate for the exam workflow. If the wrong destination was selected, correct the workflow, resend the images if appropriate, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Check Modality Worklist Query Behavior
steps.instructions: If the issue is missing or unavailable worklist exams, verify the query conditions. Confirm the correct date range is selected. Check whether the patient location, modality, scheduled station, room, or department filter is too restrictive. Search by accession number or patient name if allowed by workflow. Confirm the order exists in the RIS/EHR before assuming the Ysio Max failed. Expected outcome: You determine whether the order is missing from the source system or blocked by a query/filter mismatch. Why it matters: Many “worklist not working” calls are caused by scheduling, order status, department mismatch, or overly narrow search filters. If the order appears after correcting filters, stop and document the workflow correction.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Exam Data
steps.instructions: Check whether images are failing because of incomplete or incorrect exam information. Confirm patient name, MRN, accession number, study description, and exam status. Check for manually entered exams with missing accession numbers. Confirm whether the exam was started from worklist or entered manually. Expected outcome: Patient and exam data are complete enough for the PACS/RIS workflow. Why it matters: PACS or routing rules may reject studies with missing accession numbers, wrong modality data, or mismatched patient identifiers.

steps.title: Confirm Network Settings Were Not Recently Changed
steps.instructions: Ask whether any known changes occurred before the issue started. Check with radiology IT, PACS, or the help desk for recent changes involving: PACS server, RIS/worklist server, AE Title, IP address, port number, hostname, firewall rules, VLAN or switch replacement, certificate/security updates. Expected outcome: You identify whether the failure began after a known IT or PACS change. Why it matters: DICOM communication depends on matching network parameters between the modality and the receiving system. A server-side change can make a previously working system fail.

steps.title: Restart the Application or Workstation if Safe
steps.instructions: If no patient is active and local policy allows, perform a controlled restart. Confirm no active acquisition or unsent critical exam is at risk. Close and reopen the imaging application if available. If needed, perform a controlled workstation/system restart according to site procedure. After restart, test worklist query and DICOM send with a noncritical or completed exam. Expected outcome: Temporary application or communication lockup clears. If communication returns after restart, monitor the queue and document the recovery.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Workflow
steps.instructions: Use a controlled test to separate system failure from order/workflow issues. Have staff create or identify a valid test/scheduled exam if site policy allows. Query the worklist. Acquire or use a permitted test image if appropriate. Send to the normal PACS destination. Expected outcome: The system either communicates normally with a valid workflow or continues to fail despite correct setup. Why it matters: A known-good test prevents chasing a problem caused by one bad order, one failed study, or one incorrect destination.

steps.title: Escalate to PACS/IT When External Network Causes Are Likely
steps.instructions: If the system appears functional but cannot communicate, involve the correct support group. Provide PACS/IT with: Ysio Max room/location, system IP address if known, AE Title if known, destination name, time of failed send or query, accession number or test study used, displayed error message, whether other modalities are affected. Expected outcome: PACS/IT can check server logs, firewall rules, routing, switch status, and destination availability. Why it matters: Clinical Engineering can verify the device-side basics, but PACS/RIS/server-side failures usually require IT or imaging informatics support.

steps.title: Do Not Change DICOM Configuration Without Approval
steps.instructions: Avoid making configuration changes unless authorized and documented. Do not change AE Title, IP address, port, hostname, DICOM destination, or worklist settings casually. Only make changes when directed by approved site procedure, Siemens service, PACS administration, or validated configuration records. Expected outcome: The system configuration remains controlled and traceable. Why it matters: Incorrect DICOM changes can break routing, cause patient data issues, or affect multiple downstream systems.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Siemens Ysio Max could acquire images, but DICOM sends were failing and the Modality Worklist was not updating.
CCR.Cause: Found the system connected to an inactive network wall jack after the room network cable had been moved during cleaning.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected the Ysio Max to the correct active network jack, confirmed worklist query returned scheduled exams, resent queued images to PACS, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, communication warning, queue error, or displayed message documented
Accessories swapped, network cable condition checked, and connection point verified
Power behavior, workstation power, login state, and application readiness verified
Environmental factors, room network changes, cleaning activity, or moved cables noted
Indicator lights, wall jack status, and switch-port status verified by IT if applicable
Patient, exam, and accession information verified
Other imaging systems checked for similar issues
PACS/RIS/help desk ticket number included if applicable

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - Flat Detector Image Artifact or No Image
description: Troubleshooting flat detector image artifacts, poor image display, or no image conditions caused by setup, cabling, detector, or system communication issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot imaging failure during an active procedure unless the clinical team confirms it is safe. Confirm whether the Cios Alpha is actively being used on a patient. If image quality is poor, artifacts are present, or no image is displayed during a procedure, notify the clinical team immediately. If imaging is required for patient care, have staff move to another verified imaging system if available. Do not repeatedly expose while troubleshooting unless clinical need and radiation safety practices are followed. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on unreliable imaging. If this resolves the immediate clinical risk, stop and document the issue for follow-up.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is truly with the flat detector image path. Ask staff what they observed: no live image, no stored image, image artifacts, lines or bands, dark or washed-out image, intermittent image dropout, or image visible on one monitor but not another. Confirm whether the system powers up normally and whether the monitor cart displays menus or system messages. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to image acquisition, image display, detector communication, or monitor/display output.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Obstruction or Positioning Issues
steps.instructions: Artifacts may be caused by something in the beam path rather than a detector failure. Inspect the C-arm position and detector area. Check for metal objects in the field, table hardware, patient positioning aids, cables crossing the imaging field, lead shielding, tools or surgical instruments, and improper collimator positioning. Expected outcome: The image field is clear of unintended objects. If removing an object clears the artifact, return the unit to use after verifying image quality. Stop.

steps.title: Confirm Proper System Startup
steps.instructions: A flat detector or imaging chain issue may occur if the system did not initialize correctly. Ask whether the issue appeared after startup, transport, reboot, or repositioning. If clinically safe, power down the system using the normal shutdown process. Allow the system to fully shut down. Restart the system and allow it to complete initialization without moving the C-arm or detector. Watch for startup errors, detector communication messages, or imaging warnings. Expected outcome: The system completes startup without detector or imaging errors. If rebooting restores normal imaging and no error returns, document the reset and return to service after functional verification. Stop.

steps.title: Check Detector and C-Arm Physical Condition
steps.instructions: The flat detector is a critical and expensive imaging component. Physical damage should be ruled out early. Inspect the flat detector housing for cracks, impact marks, loose covers, fluid intrusion, damaged edges, signs of collision, unusual heat, or burning smell. Check whether the detector was recently struck, bumped, or forced into position. Expected outcome: No visible damage or unsafe condition is found. If damage, fluid intrusion, unusual heat, or burning smell is present, remove the system from service immediately. Stop.

steps.title: Verify Cable Connections Between the C-Arm and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: No image or intermittent image may be caused by loose, damaged, or poorly seated system cables. Inspect external cables between the C-arm stand and monitor cart. Confirm connectors are fully seated and locked if applicable. Look for bent pins, loose strain relief, damaged cable jacket, crushed cable sections, tension on connectors, or cables caught under wheels. Do not force connectors. Expected outcome: All external image/system cables are secure and undamaged. If reseating a loose external cable restores the image, perform a test exposure or image check according to site practice. Stop.

steps.title: Check Monitor Cart Display Function
steps.instructions: A “no image” complaint may be a display issue rather than a detector issue. Confirm the monitor cart is powered on. Check whether system menus, patient/exam information, stored images, or status screens are visible. Determine whether both monitors are blank, only one monitor is blank, menus appear but live image does not, or live image appears but stored images do not. Adjust brightness only if appropriate and verify the display is not simply dimmed. Expected outcome: Display failure is separated from detector or acquisition failure. If one display is failed but the system is otherwise imaging normally, remove from service or limit use according to site policy and repair the display issue.

steps.title: Check Exposure Readiness and Imaging Mode
steps.instructions: The system may appear to have no image if it is not ready to expose or is in an unexpected imaging mode. Confirm the system is not in standby, service mode, review-only mode, or another non-imaging state. Verify the selected imaging mode is appropriate for the clinical use. Check whether the footswitch or hand switch is being recognized. Confirm the system gives normal exposure-ready indications. Expected outcome: The system is ready for imaging and responds normally to exposure controls. If the issue is caused by an incorrect mode or standby condition, correct the setting and verify imaging. Stop.

steps.title: Inspect Footswitch or Hand Switch Operation
steps.instructions: If no image is produced, the system may not be receiving an exposure command. Check whether the footswitch or hand switch is connected properly. Inspect the cable and connector for damage. Try an alternate approved exposure switch if available and allowed by site practice. Confirm whether the system responds when the switch is activated. Expected outcome: Exposure controls are confirmed functional or ruled out as the cause. If a faulty switch is found, remove the switch from service and replace it with a known-good approved accessory. Stop.

steps.title: Check for System Error Messages
steps.instructions: Detector communication or image processing errors may appear on the monitor cart. Review any active system messages, warning icons, or error codes. Look for messages related to flat detector, image acquisition, image processing, generator readiness, monitor cart communication, system cable connection, temperature, calibration or correction failure. Record the exact wording of any error message. Expected outcome: Any system-reported fault is captured accurately for repair documentation. If a specific detector, generator, or image processing error remains active after basic checks, remove the system from service.

steps.title: Compare Live Image Versus Stored Image Behavior
steps.instructions: This helps narrow the issue between acquisition, processing, and display. If safe and appropriate, review previously stored images. Compare whether stored images display normally, but live image is missing; live image appears, but saved images are corrupted; both live and stored images show artifacts; or only new images show artifacts. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to display, acquisition, storage, or image processing. If only new images are affected, suspect the current acquisition chain, detector, calibration/correction, or processing path.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Handling Causes
steps.instructions: Some image artifacts may follow transport, collision, temperature change, or detector stress. Ask whether the unit was recently moved between rooms, bumped, cleaned, exposed to fluids, or stored in an unusual environment. Check that the system has been allowed to stabilize if it was moved from a cold or hot area. Confirm the detector area is clean and dry. Do not apply cleaners directly to seams, detector surfaces, connectors, or vents. Expected outcome: Obvious environmental or handling causes are identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Image Check
steps.instructions: Only perform this when it is safe and allowed by facility policy. Use an approved test object or site-accepted imaging check process. Acquire a test image. Look for uniform image field, no fixed lines or bands, no blank areas, no repeated artifacts, no image freezing, no delayed image update, and normal storage or review function. Expected outcome: The system produces a stable, usable image without artifacts. If the test image is normal and no errors remain, document findings and return the unit to service. Stop.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Internal Detector or Imaging Chain Repair at Point of Use
steps.instructions: The flat detector, generator, image processor, and internal communication assemblies require proper service procedures. Do not open detector housings. Do not bypass interlocks. Do not attempt internal board-level repair in the clinical area. Do not continue using the system if artifacts could affect clinical interpretation. Expected outcome: The system is handled safely and escalated appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the Siemens Cios Alpha had a flat detector image artifact and intermittently produced no live image during use.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found the monitor cart communication cable was not fully seated after transport, causing intermittent image dropout.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and secured the external system cable, restarted the Cios Alpha, verified normal startup, acquired a test image, and confirmed no artifacts or image loss.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, exact error message, or warning displayed
Accessories swapped, including footswitch, hand switch, monitor cable, or approved external cable
Power behavior, including whether the system powered on normally or failed startup
Environmental factors, including whether the issue followed transport, cleaning, collision, cold storage, heat exposure, or fluid exposure
Indicator lights, monitor cart display behavior, startup warnings, detector warnings, or communication status messages
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - Generator / Exposure Inhibit Fault
description: Troubleshooting Cios Alpha exposure inhibit faults caused by safety interlocks, controls, generator readiness, overheating, or accessory issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot exposure or generator faults during an active procedure unless clinical leadership confirms it is safe. Confirm whether the C-arm is actively needed for patient care. If imaging is required immediately, have clinical staff move to another available C-arm or imaging method. Keep staff clear of the radiation field. Do not attempt repeated exposure attempts if the system is inhibiting radiation. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on a C-arm that may not expose properly. Why it matters: An exposure inhibit is a safety-related condition. Repeated exposure attempts can delay care and may indicate the system is intentionally blocking radiation release.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message or behavior reported by staff. Ask whether the issue occurs with: Fluoro, Single shot, Subtraction or vascular run, Roadmap or advanced imaging mode, Footswitch exposure, Handswitch exposure, Table-side controls. Observe the system display for any active warning, inhibit, generator, temperature, interlock, or readiness message. Expected outcome: The fault is confirmed and tied to a specific exposure mode or control method. Why it matters: A C-arm that will not expose from one control may have a control/accessory issue, while failure in all modes may point toward a system readiness, interlock, generator, or internal fault.

steps.title: Check That the System Is Fully Ready
steps.instructions: Confirm the Cios Alpha has completed startup and is not still initializing. Verify the system is powered on normally. Check that the monitor cart and C-arm are communicating. Confirm the user interface is responsive. Look for any boot, initialization, system readiness, or application loading messages. Expected outcome: The system reaches a normal ready state before exposure is attempted. If the system finishes startup and exposure becomes available, return the unit to service after functional testing and documentation.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Radiation Lockout Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that no emergency stop or radiation lockout condition is active. Inspect the emergency stop buttons on the system. Verify none are pressed or latched. Confirm any radiation release lockout feature is not active. Ask staff whether an emergency stop was recently pressed. Expected outcome: Emergency stop and radiation lockout conditions are cleared. Why it matters: Mobile C-arms may intentionally block movement or radiation release when emergency stop or radiation lockout conditions are active.

steps.title: Check Footswitch and Handswitch Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the radiation release controls. Verify the footswitch is connected securely. Check for damaged cable insulation, crushed sections, bent pins, loose connectors, or fluid contamination. Confirm the handswitch, if used, is connected and undamaged. Try a known-good compatible control accessory if available and approved by your department. Test whether exposure works from an alternate radiation release control. Expected outcome: Exposure works with a known-good control or from an alternate control method. If replacing or reseating the footswitch/handswitch resolves the issue, document the faulty accessory and remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Stuck or Partially Pressed Controls
steps.instructions: Look for controls that may be stuck, pressed, or physically damaged. Inspect the footswitch pedals. Check hand controls, table-side controls, and monitor cart controls. Confirm no button is jammed, taped down, contaminated, or mechanically stuck. Power cycle only if safe and permitted by local workflow. Expected outcome: Controls return to normal and the system no longer inhibits exposure. Why it matters: A stuck control can prevent the system from recognizing a valid new exposure command.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Imaging Mode and Operator Setup
steps.instructions: Check whether the system is in a valid mode for exposure. Verify the selected imaging mode is appropriate. Confirm the system is not in review-only, image playback, setup, reference image, standby, or non-exposure mode. Have the operator select a basic fluoro mode and attempt exposure only when safe. Expected outcome: Exposure is available in a normal imaging mode. If basic fluoro works but a specific advanced mode does not, document the failed mode and escalate if needed.

steps.title: Check C-Arm Position and Mechanical Locks
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is positioned safely and not in a condition that prevents exposure. Check that the C-arm is not against a hard stop or collision point. Verify brakes and locks are being used appropriately. Inspect for obvious collision, obstruction, or mechanical stress. Confirm the detector and tube are not blocked or physically damaged. Expected outcome: The C-arm is positioned safely and the system is not inhibiting exposure due to position or collision-related concerns.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Line Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the system has stable power. Verify the power cord is fully seated. Inspect the cord and plug for damage. Confirm the wall outlet is hospital-grade and functioning. Try a known-good outlet if safe and practical. Avoid extension cords or shared power strips. Check whether the fault occurs only during high-demand exposure modes. Expected outcome: The C-arm operates on a stable power source without exposure inhibit. Why it matters: Generator-related exposure problems can appear when the system does not have adequate or stable input power.

steps.title: Check Battery or Energy Storage Status if Applicable
steps.instructions: Review the system power status. Check for low battery, charging, energy storage, or power module warnings. Allow the system to charge if the display indicates insufficient stored energy. Confirm the fault is not occurring because the system is not ready for the selected exposure demand. Expected outcome: Exposure is allowed once the system has adequate power readiness.

steps.title: Check Tube or Generator Temperature Warnings
steps.instructions: Look for thermal warnings or cooling-related messages. Check the display for tube heat, generator heat, cooling, or temperature warnings. Ask staff whether the issue occurred after heavy fluoro use, vascular runs, or long procedures. Allow the system to cool if a thermal limit warning is present. Confirm cooling fans or obvious external airflow paths are not blocked. Expected outcome: Exposure becomes available after the system returns within safe thermal limits. Why it matters: The Cios Alpha is a high-power mobile C-arm, and thermal protection may inhibit exposure to protect the tube, generator, or cooling system.

steps.title: Check Visible Cables Between C-Arm and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: Inspect external communication and power connections. Check visible system cables for looseness, damage, strain, or poor seating. Look for pinched cables near wheels, cart connection points, arm joints, or storage hooks. Confirm connectors are fully seated where user-accessible and appropriate. Do not open covers or access internal generator wiring. Expected outcome: Communication and system readiness return after correcting an external cable issue.

steps.title: Check for Related Error Messages
steps.instructions: Look beyond the exposure inhibit message. Document all active alarms, faults, or status messages. Look for messages related to: Generator, Tube, Cooling, Detector, Footswitch, Handswitch, Power, Communication, Emergency stop, Radiation release. Expected outcome: The root symptom is better defined before escalation. Why it matters: “Exposure inhibit” may be the result of another active system fault rather than the primary failure.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Test
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, perform a controlled test. Confirm no patient is in the beam path. Use department-approved radiation safety practices. Verify fluoro exposure starts and stops normally. Confirm image appears on the monitor. Confirm exposure indicators and audible/visual radiation signals work as expected. Expected outcome: The C-arm exposes normally and imaging function is restored. If the unit passes functional testing, return it to service and document the action taken.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported that the Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha would not produce fluoro exposure and displayed a generator/exposure inhibit fault.
CCR.Cause: Found the footswitch cable partially seated, preventing valid radiation release recognition.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the footswitch connection, verified no active inhibit messages remained, performed safe fluoro functional test, and returned the C-arm to service.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sound, smell, heat, or fan behavior
Whether the issue followed heavy fluoro or vascular use
Whether a known-good accessory was swapped

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - Touchscreen Or System Control Failure
description: Troubleshooting touchscreen, control panel, or system input problems affecting normal C-arm operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the C-arm is not being used for an active procedure. Confirm with clinical staff whether imaging is currently needed. If the unit is in use during a case, do not restart, unplug, or change controls unless directed by the clinical team and it is safe to do so. If imaging is urgently needed and the C-arm cannot be controlled reliably, move to a backup C-arm if available. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on a C-arm with unreliable controls. Why it matters: A touchscreen or control failure can prevent positioning, image acquisition, dose control, or system shutdown commands.
