steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what part of the control system is not responding. Check whether the issue affects: Touchscreen only, Physical buttons only, Both touchscreen and physical controls, Monitor cart controls, C-arm stand controls, Footswitch operation, System boot or login screen. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to a touchscreen, control panel, cart, stand, or system-wide issue. If only one control area is affected, continue troubleshooting that area. If the entire system is frozen, proceed as a system control failure.

steps.title: Check for an Active Procedure Lock, Mode Selection, or User Prompt
steps.instructions: Look for any message, confirmation box, safety prompt, login screen, or mode selection that may prevent normal control. Review the touchscreen and monitor display for prompts requiring confirmation. Check whether the system is waiting for a user login, exam selection, patient selection, or procedure mode. Confirm whether fluoroscopy, image review, or system movement is disabled by a selected mode or workflow step. Expected outcome: If the system was waiting for a required selection, complete the prompt and verify control returns. If this resolves the issue, document the user interface condition and return the unit to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Check for anything interfering with touch recognition. Inspect the screen for fluid, gel, cleaning residue, tape, drape material, cracked glass, heavy scratches, or pressure from attached accessories. Clean the touchscreen using the facility-approved method. Remove anything touching the screen edge or resting against the display. Expected outcome: The touchscreen responds normally after obstruction or contamination is removed. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the external cause.

steps.title: Check for Glove or Stylus Interaction Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the screen responds differently depending on how it is touched. Have a trained operator test the screen with a clean dry finger if permitted by infection control policy. Check whether gloves are wet, heavily powdered, double-layered, or contaminated with gel or fluids. Do not use sharp objects, pens, tools, or metal probes on the touchscreen. Expected outcome: If the touchscreen responds with proper contact, the issue may be user interaction or contamination related. If the screen remains unresponsive, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Physical Control Buttons and Emergency Stop Status
steps.instructions: Check whether physical system controls are functional. Test available non-imaging controls only when safe. Check whether the emergency stop is activated. Confirm that any emergency stop button is fully released according to normal user operation. Inspect control buttons for sticking, damage, missing caps, or fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: Physical controls should respond normally unless the system is locked, frozen, or has a control hardware problem. If emergency stop was active and releasing it restores control, verify safe operation before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check Footswitch and Hand Control Accessories
steps.instructions: A connected accessory can sometimes appear like a control failure if it is damaged, stuck, disconnected, or not recognized. Inspect the footswitch cable and connector for damage, bent pins, looseness, or contamination. Confirm the footswitch is connected to the correct port. Check whether any footswitch pedal appears stuck or physically damaged. If a known-good compatible footswitch is available, swap it only if allowed by facility process. Expected outcome: The system recognizes control accessories and no stuck input is present. If a defective accessory is found, remove it from service and test the C-arm with a known-good accessory.

steps.title: Check Cables Between the C-Arm Stand and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: A communication issue between the imaging stand and monitor cart may cause missing controls, frozen screens, or partial system response. Inspect external interconnect cables between the stand and monitor cart. Confirm connectors are fully seated and latched. Look for crushed cables, stretched cables, bent pins, or strain at connector ends. Avoid forcing connectors. Expected outcome: External communication cables are secure and undamaged. If reseating a loose cable restores normal control, perform a functional check before returning the system to service.

steps.title: Confirm Power Stability
steps.instructions: Touchscreen and system control failures can occur if the system has unstable power or incomplete startup. Verify the C-arm is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and undamaged. Confirm there are no loose plugs, damaged outlet faces, tripped breakers, or power strips being used improperly. If safe and appropriate, test the outlet or use a verified outlet. Expected outcome: The system has stable AC power and is not affected by an external power issue. If power instability is found, correct the power source issue and retest.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart If Safe
steps.instructions: If the system is frozen or the touchscreen is locked up, a controlled restart may clear a temporary software state. Confirm the C-arm is not needed for an active procedure. Notify clinical staff before restarting. Use the normal shutdown process if the controls allow it. If the system is completely frozen, follow facility-approved power cycling practice. Allow the system to fully shut down before restarting. Expected outcome: The system restarts normally, the touchscreen responds, and controls function after boot. If the issue clears after restart, perform a basic operational check before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Freezing or Delayed Response
steps.instructions: A one-time software freeze may clear, but repeated failures should not be ignored. Observe whether the touchscreen responds slowly, intermittently, or only after long delays. Check whether the failure occurs during startup, exam selection, image acquisition, image review, or shutdown. Ask staff whether the issue happens after the unit has been on for a long time. Note any error messages, system warnings, or abnormal sounds. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented clearly. If the issue repeats, remove the C-arm from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the C-arm to service, verify that normal controls are working. Confirm touchscreen response across multiple areas of the screen. Verify physical controls respond appropriately. Confirm the monitor cart and C-arm stand communicate normally. Check system startup, shutdown, exam workflow, image review controls, and basic movement controls where safe. Do not perform radiation testing unless authorized and conditions are appropriate. Expected outcome: The system controls are responsive, stable, and safe for clinical use. If any control remains unreliable, do not return the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha touchscreen was not responding and system controls were intermittently frozen during setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no active procedure, no screen obstruction, stable AC power, and secure stand-to-cart connections, but the control failure returned after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-arm from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the repeated touchscreen/control failure, and escalated the unit for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether monitor cart controls or stand controls failed
Cable condition between stand and cart
Whether failure was intermittent or constant

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - DICOM/PACS Export Failure
description: Troubleshooting Cios Alpha DICOM or PACS export failures caused by network, destination, patient data, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the C-arm is not actively needed for a procedure. Do not troubleshoot DICOM export during an active case unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. Confirm images needed for patient care are still visible locally on the Cios Alpha. If imaging is urgently needed, allow the clinical team to continue using the system locally while export troubleshooting waits. Do not delete studies, exams, or patient data during troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely, and stored images are protected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Export Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is failing. Ask whether the issue affects one study, one patient, one destination, or all exports. Check whether the export fails immediately, stays queued, times out, or reports a specific DICOM/network error. Confirm whether images are stored locally on the C-arm. Try exporting a non-critical test or completed study if permitted by site policy. Expected outcome: You identify whether this is a single-study issue, destination issue, or system-wide export problem. If export works for other studies or destinations, stop broad troubleshooting and focus on the affected study, patient data, or destination.

steps.title: Confirm the C-Arm Is Connected to the Network
steps.instructions: Check the simplest external network causes first. Verify the network cable is connected securely to the Cios Alpha or docking/network connection point. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct wall jack or network switch port. Look for link/activity lights at the network jack, switch, or adapter if visible. If the system uses a docking station, cart interface, or hospital network drop, reseat the connection. Expected outcome: The C-arm has a valid physical network connection. If a loose or disconnected cable is found, reconnect it and retry export. If the export succeeds, document the cable/network connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Network Drop or Cable
steps.instructions: Rule out a bad cable, jack, or local network path. Swap the Ethernet cable with a known-good cable if available. Move to a known-working network jack approved for imaging/PACS traffic if practical. Avoid using random public or non-clinical network ports unless IT confirms they are configured correctly. Confirm whether other imaging devices on the same network area are exporting normally. Expected outcome: The export either works on a known-good connection or continues to fail. If the export works after changing the cable or network drop, the original cable, jack, or network path is the likely cause. Stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Check for Local Network Address Problems
steps.instructions: Confirm the system appears to have normal network connectivity. Review the C-arm network status/settings if accessible under normal user or service-permitted menus. Look for missing IP address, incorrect subnet, gateway issue, or disconnected network status. Confirm whether the device is configured for DHCP or static IP according to site standards. Compare the displayed network information to the hospital’s expected configuration, if available. Expected outcome: The C-arm has a valid network identity for the imaging network. If the IP address is missing, incorrect, duplicated, or outside the expected range, escalate to IT/PACS support or approved service support before changing network configuration.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct DICOM/PACS Destination Is Selected
steps.instructions: A wrong destination can look like a device failure. Verify the selected export destination is the correct PACS, archive, workstation, or DICOM node. Confirm the destination name matches the site workflow. If multiple destinations exist, try a known-good destination if permitted. Ask staff whether a recent PACS, network, or destination change occurred. Expected outcome: The system is attempting export to the correct destination. If another DICOM destination works, the issue is likely destination-specific rather than a total C-arm failure.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Study Information
steps.instructions: Incorrect or incomplete patient/study data can interfere with DICOM workflow. Confirm the patient name, patient ID/MRN, accession number, study date, and modality information are entered correctly. Check for unusual characters, missing required fields, duplicate patient entries, or mismatched accession numbers. Confirm whether the study was created through the expected worklist process or manually entered. If the problem is limited to one patient or exam, compare it with a study that exported successfully. Expected outcome: Required DICOM fields are complete and reasonable. If correcting patient/study information allows export, document the data issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Modality Worklist or Order Association
steps.instructions: If the site uses modality worklist, export may depend on proper order selection. Confirm the exam was selected from the correct worklist entry when applicable. Verify the accession number and patient identifiers match the order in the hospital system. Ask clinical staff whether the case was performed under an emergency/manual entry workflow. If the worklist is not loading or patient data does not match, involve PACS/RIS/IT support. Expected outcome: The exported study is properly associated with the correct order. If the study exports after proper order association, the issue was likely workflow or demographic mismatch.

steps.title: Review the Export Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether studies are stuck, pending, failed, or repeatedly retrying. Open the export, send, archive, or network queue if available. Look for failed studies, repeated retries, or a backlog of unsent exams. Confirm whether all studies are stuck or only one study is failing. Do not clear, delete, or purge queued studies unless approved by clinical leadership and site policy. Expected outcome: You determine whether the problem is a queue backlog, a single failed study, or total DICOM communication failure. If one study is blocking the queue, escalate to PACS/IT or vendor support before deleting or altering data.

steps.title: Confirm PACS or DICOM Destination Availability
steps.instructions: The C-arm may be functioning normally while the receiving system is unavailable. Ask PACS/IT whether the destination is online and accepting DICOM studies. Confirm whether other modalities are sending successfully to the same destination. Verify whether there was recent PACS maintenance, network maintenance, firewall change, or server migration. Check whether the destination AE Title, IP address, and port may have changed. Expected outcome: The receiving PACS/DICOM node is confirmed available. If other devices also cannot export, the issue is likely PACS, network, or destination-side rather than the Cios Alpha.

steps.title: Check for Recent Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue started after a change. Ask staff when export last worked. Check whether the C-arm was moved to a different room, network jack, or OR. Ask whether the hospital changed PACS, VLANs, firewall rules, IP ranges, AE Titles, or ports. Review recent service activity, software updates, or network configuration changes. Expected outcome: You identify whether the failure began after a known environmental or configuration change. If the issue began after a network or PACS change, coordinate with IT/PACS and vendor support as needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: A restart may clear a temporary software or communication hang, but it should not interrupt care. Confirm the C-arm is not being used for an active procedure. Verify images are stored locally before restarting. Perform a normal shutdown and restart according to site procedure. After restart, confirm the system boots normally and retry export. Expected outcome: Temporary software or network session issues may clear. If export succeeds after restart, monitor for recurrence and document the restart as the corrective action.

steps.title: Escalate for DICOM Configuration or Internal Fault Evaluation
steps.instructions: Do not guess or make unauthorized DICOM configuration changes. Escalate to PACS/IT if network reachability, AE Title, IP address, port, firewall, worklist, or destination availability is suspected. Escalate to Siemens service or approved imaging service support if the C-arm appears to have a software, storage, DICOM service, or internal communication issue. Preserve failed export details, error messages, timestamps, patient/study identifiers, and destination name for support. Expected outcome: The correct support team receives enough information to isolate the failure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-arm would not export images to PACS after a completed case.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the C-arm connected to a network wall jack that was not configured for the imaging/PACS network.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected the C-arm to an approved imaging network port, verified local image storage, successfully exported a test study to PACS, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected one study or all studies
Date and time of failed export
Accessories swapped, including Ethernet cable, docking connection, or network adapter
Power behavior before and after restart
Environmental factors, including room, network jack, OR, or recent relocation
Network jack or room location used
Whether PACS/IT or Siemens service was contacted

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - Cooling / Thermal Warning During Use
description: Troubleshooting cooling or thermal warnings during C-arm use caused by heat buildup, airflow restriction, workload, or environmental conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the procedure can continue safely. Confirm with the clinical team whether imaging is urgently needed. If the warning affects imaging availability, dose delivery, or system operation, have another imaging option available if possible. Do not power cycle, move, or service the C-arm while it is actively needed for patient care unless the clinical team confirms it is safe. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before the thermal issue is investigated. Why it matters: A cooling or thermal warning may progress to limited imaging, automatic shutdown, or interrupted fluoroscopy.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Warning
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact warning message displayed on the Cios Alpha. Check whether the warning appears on the main system display, monitor cart, generator interface, or system status area. Ask staff when the warning appeared: During continuous fluoro. After repeated exposures. During startup. After the unit was moved. After being stored in a warm area. Expected outcome: The thermal warning is confirmed and the operating conditions are understood. If the message clears and does not return after normal cool-down, document the event and return the unit to service if all functions pass.

steps.title: Stop Imaging Long Enough to Allow Cooling
steps.instructions: Pause imaging if clinically acceptable. Ask staff to stop fluoroscopy or exposure use while keeping the system powered as appropriate. Allow the system time to cool with normal fans operating. Observe whether the warning changes, clears, or escalates. Expected outcome: A workload-related temperature warning should improve after reduced use. If the warning clears and imaging resumes normally, the issue was likely related to heat buildup from heavy use. Document and stop.

steps.title: Check for Blocked Airflow
steps.instructions: Inspect the C-arm and monitor cart for blocked vents or restricted airflow. Look for drapes, covers, linens, bags, wall clearance issues, or equipment carts blocking ventilation openings. Make sure the system is not pushed tightly against a wall or cabinet. Verify that the system has enough space around cooling vents. Expected outcome: Airflow paths are open and unrestricted. If blocked airflow is found, remove the obstruction, allow the system to cool, and retest operation.

steps.title: Check Room Conditions
steps.instructions: Evaluate whether the room environment may be contributing to overheating. Check if the room is unusually warm. Confirm the unit is not positioned near warming devices, direct sunlight, heat exhaust, or other equipment generating heat. Ask whether the system was recently moved from a hot storage area into the procedure room. Expected outcome: The system is operating in a reasonable clinical environment with no obvious external heat source. If environmental heat appears to be the cause, allow the system to stabilize in the room before returning it to active use.

steps.title: Listen for Cooling Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Without opening covers, listen for normal fan operation. Stand near the system base, generator area, and monitor cart. Listen for normal fan noise. Check for unusual sounds such as grinding, rattling, intermittent fan cycling, or no fan operation when the unit is warm. Expected outcome: Cooling fans sound normal and consistent. If fan noise is abnormal, absent, or unusually loud, remove the device from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Dust or External Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible exterior vents and filters, if externally accessible. Look for dust buildup, lint, plastic wrap, tape, or debris on exterior vent areas. Do not remove internal covers unless permitted by your facility procedure and service documentation. Clean only external surfaces using approved methods. Expected outcome: Exterior cooling paths are visibly clear. If vents are heavily contaminated or the warning returns after cleaning, remove the unit from service for bench or vendor evaluation.

steps.title: Review Use Pattern With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Determine whether the warning occurred during unusually demanding imaging use. Ask whether the case involved long fluoro time, repeated high-dose imaging, large patient body habitus, steep angles, or extended continuous operation. Check whether staff noticed the warning after a specific exposure pattern. Expected outcome: You can determine whether the warning is likely due to normal thermal protection during heavy use. If the unit only warns after unusually heavy use and recovers normally, document the workload condition and educate staff to allow cooling breaks when clinically possible.

steps.title: Check System Startup and Self-Test Behavior
steps.instructions: After the system has cooled, restart only if clinically safe and allowed by facility procedure. Power the system down normally. Wait briefly before restarting. Observe startup behavior, self-test messages, fan behavior, and whether the thermal warning returns immediately. Expected outcome: A heat-related warning should not return immediately after proper cool-down. If the thermal warning appears immediately at startup while the system is cool, suspect a temperature sensor, cooling control, fan, generator, power, or internal electronics issue.

steps.title: Perform Basic Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Once the warning clears, perform a limited operational check according to site policy. Confirm the system powers normally. Verify monitor display and image chain operation. Confirm C-arm movement is normal. Check that no cooling, temperature, generator, or system error returns. Perform a safe test exposure only if permitted and appropriate. Expected outcome: The system operates normally without recurring thermal warning. If the warning returns quickly under normal use, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported a cooling/thermal warning on the Siemens Cios Alpha during fluoroscopy use.
CCR.Cause: Found the warning occurred after extended imaging use with restricted airflow near the system vents.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared airflow obstruction, allowed system to cool, verified normal startup and imaging function, and returned the C-arm to service.

helpfulDetails: Room temperature or unusual heat conditions
Approximate fluoro time before warning appeared
Whether warning occurred during continuous or repeated imaging
Any unusual smell, heat, or noise
Whether the warning cleared after cool-down
Whether warning returned immediately at startup

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Aera MRI System - Coil Not Recognized / Coil Element Failure
description: Troubleshooting MRI coil recognition, coil connection, coil element dropout, or scan-start failures caused by external coil or setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the issue occurs during patient setup or scanning, stop the exam only when clinically safe and follow MRI department workflow. Remove the patient from the bore or move to a safe position before handling coils, cables, table connections, or accessories. Expected outcome: The patient is not exposed to unnecessary delays, repositioning risk, or unsafe coil handling.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Coil Issue
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff which coil was in use and what symptom occurred. Common reports may include: Coil not detected; Coil element missing; Coil element failed; Coil cannot be selected; Exam will not start; Poor signal from one area of the image; Error appears after patient positioning. Expected outcome: The failure is tied to a specific coil, coil port, exam setup, or scan protocol.

steps.title: Check Whether the Correct Coil Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the selected coil matches the exam type, body region, patient position, and protocol. Confirm that the coil is compatible with the MAGNETOM Aera system and not intended for another scanner or platform. Expected outcome: The coil and exam setup match the intended clinical use. If the wrong coil was selected or positioned, correct the setup and retest. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Coil Exterior
steps.instructions: Remove the coil from patient use and visually inspect it in a safe MRI area. Look for: Cracked housing; Damaged strain relief; Bent connector pins; Loose connector shell; Fluid intrusion; Burn marks; Unusual odor; Damaged labels or missing coil ID markings. Expected outcome: No obvious physical damage is found. If physical damage, overheating, fluid exposure, or connector damage is found, remove the coil from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check Coil Connector Seating
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the coil using normal operator-level handling only. Ensure the connector is fully seated and latched as designed. Do not force the connector. Expected outcome: The scanner recognizes the coil after a clean reconnection. If reseating the connector resolves the issue, perform a basic function check or phantom/QA verification per site process before returning the coil to use.

steps.title: Inspect the Table or Coil Port Connection Area
steps.instructions: Check the table connection interface and coil ports for visible debris, bent contacts, loose hardware, fluid, or signs of wear. Do not insert tools into the connector or attempt internal port repair. Expected outcome: The connection area appears clean, dry, and undamaged. If the table port or connector area appears damaged, remove the affected port or scanner from clinical use as appropriate and escalate for service.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Coil on the Same Port
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible coil and connect it to the same table or system port. Expected outcome: The known-good coil is recognized normally. If the known-good coil works, the original coil is more likely the problem. If the known-good coil also fails on the same port, suspect a table, port, system interface, or software recognition issue.

steps.title: Try the Suspect Coil on a Known-Good Port or Setup
steps.instructions: If the coil design allows connection to another appropriate port or setup, test the suspect coil there. Expected outcome: The suspect coil is either recognized or fails consistently. If the coil fails in multiple valid setups, remove the coil from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Coil Element Dropout During QA or Phantom Testing
steps.instructions: If the coil is recognized but image quality is poor, perform a controlled phantom or coil QA check according to department workflow. Look for: Missing signal from one coil section; Shading; Localized noise; Intermittent signal loss; Repeatable artifact tied to the same coil element. Expected outcome: All expected coil elements contribute normally. If a specific element repeatedly fails, remove the coil from clinical use and document the failed element behavior.

steps.title: Confirm Protocol and Coil Selection Settings
steps.instructions: Review whether the exam card, coil selection, patient orientation, and scan setup are appropriate. Incorrect coil selection or patient orientation can make a good coil appear unavailable or produce poor signal. Expected outcome: The system setup matches the physical coil and patient positioning. If correcting the protocol or coil selection resolves the issue, document the setup correction and return the device to use.

steps.title: Restart the System Only if Operationally Appropriate
steps.instructions: If multiple coils are not recognized or the scanner interface appears stuck, coordinate with MRI staff before restarting the host or scanner software. Do not interrupt active clinical use without department approval. Expected outcome: Temporary software recognition faults clear after restart. If the issue clears after restart, perform a verification scan or QA check before returning to normal use.

steps.title: Review Error Pattern and Scope
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue affects: One coil only; One coil port only; One table position only; Multiple coils; Multiple exams; Recognition only; Image quality only; Intermittent movement-related failures. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to coil, cable/connector, table interface, software recognition, or internal system fault.

steps.title: Remove Any Suspect Coil from Service
steps.instructions: If a coil is intermittently recognized, has failed elements, shows damage, or causes repeatable artifacts, label it Out of Service. Do not return an intermittent MRI coil to clinical use based only on one successful reconnect. Expected outcome: Unreliable coil performance is prevented from affecting patient imaging.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported that the Siemens MAGNETOM Aera head/neck coil was not recognized during patient setup and the exam could not proceed.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the coil failed recognition on a known-good table port while another compatible coil was recognized normally.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the suspect coil from service, labeled it Out of Service, notified MRI staff, and escalated the coil for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue was recognition, element dropout, or image artifact
Whether a known-good coil was tested
Whether the issue followed the coil or stayed with the port
Any visible coil or connector damage
Phantom or QA result, if performed
Whether the issue was intermittent or repeatable

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Aera MRI System - Patient Table Dock, Movement, or Home-Position Fault
description: Troubleshooting MAGNETOM Aera patient table docking, movement, home-position, drive, alignment, or table communication issues from external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the table is occupied, do not continue troubleshooting table motion until the patient is safe. Move the patient off the table if table movement is unreliable, unexpected, obstructed, or unable to reach the required scan position. Expected outcome: Patient safety is maintained before mechanical or positioning troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Table Fault
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what happened and when the fault occurred. Check whether the issue involves: Table will not dock; Table will not undock; Table will not move in or out; Table will not raise or lower; Table does not reach home position; Table stops during movement; Table position is not recognized by the system; Error appears during patient setup or scan preparation. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or internal faults are suspected.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction Around the Table
steps.instructions: Inspect the table path, docking area, table base, floor, rails, and surrounding equipment. Remove any linens, straps, pads, coils, carts, footrests, step stools, cables, or patient accessories that may interfere with movement or docking. Expected outcome: Table movement path is clear. If obstruction was the cause and table operation returns to normal, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Table Is Properly Aligned for Docking
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is straight, fully seated, and aligned with the scanner docking interface. A table that is slightly misaligned may not latch, communicate, or be recognized correctly. Expected outcome: Table docks smoothly and the system recognizes the docked position. If realignment resolves the issue, return the device to service after functional checks.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Contacts and External Connection Points
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the table docking interface for obvious debris, damage, bent contact areas, loose covers, or contamination. Do not probe internal contacts or disassemble the docking assembly. Expected outcome: Docking surfaces appear clean, intact, and unobstructed. If obvious external debris is found, remove it only if safe and appropriate.

steps.title: Check Table Locks, Brakes, and Release Mechanisms
steps.instructions: Verify any table lock, brake, release pedal, or dock/undock control is in the correct position. Confirm the table is not mechanically locked out by a release handle, emergency release, or transport condition. Expected outcome: Table controls and locks are in normal operating position.

steps.title: Verify Power and System Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm the MRI system is powered, the host is responsive, and no active emergency stop or system inhibit condition is present. Check for visible system messages that may prevent table motion, such as door/interlock, emergency stop, scan-room status, or system-not-ready conditions. Expected outcome: Table movement is not being blocked by a system readiness or safety interlock condition.

steps.title: Check the Operator Controls
steps.instructions: Attempt table movement using the normal control method used by the site, such as the table-side controls, console controls, or system interface. Compare whether one control location works while another does not. Expected outcome: If one control works and another does not, document the failed control location and remove the device from full clinical use until evaluated.

steps.title: Verify Home-Position Behavior
steps.instructions: Attempt to return the table to home position using normal system controls. Observe whether the table: Moves but does not complete home position; Does not move at all; Stops short; Moves unevenly; Reports an incorrect position; Requires repeated attempts. Expected outcome: Table reaches home position and the system recognizes it correctly. If the home-position fault clears after normal repositioning, perform a repeat functional check before returning to service.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Coil Interference
steps.instructions: Remove nonessential accessories from the table, including pads, positioning aids, coil holders, immobilization devices, and loose cables. Confirm coils and cables are routed correctly and not catching during table travel. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth and unrestricted with accessories removed or properly routed.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid, Damage, Unusual Noise, Heat, or Smell
steps.instructions: Look for signs of spilled fluids, impact damage, cracked covers, damaged wheels, dragging components, abnormal grinding, burning smell, or unusual warmth near table drive areas. Do not continue operating the table if there are signs of mechanical damage or electrical overheating. Expected outcome: No unsafe condition is present. If abnormal noise, heat, smell, or damage is found, remove the table/system from service.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check
steps.instructions: With no patient on the table, test basic movement functions through a limited, controlled range. Confirm docking, undocking, longitudinal movement, vertical movement if applicable, and home-position recognition. Expected outcome: Table moves smoothly, stops correctly, docks securely, and reports position correctly.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and System Logs Available to Clinical Engineering
steps.instructions: Record the exact error message, fault code, timing, and table behavior. Avoid clearing errors repeatedly without documenting them, especially if the fault returns. Expected outcome: Enough information is captured to support vendor service or bench evaluation if the issue persists.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Device Can Safely Return to Service
steps.instructions: Return the system to service only if the table docks, moves, reaches home position, and is recognized consistently without abnormal noise, obstruction, or repeat errors. Expected outcome: Device status is based on repeatable safe operation, not a single successful movement.

CCR.Complaint: MRI technologist reported the Siemens MAGNETOM Aera patient table would not dock and the system would not recognize the table at home position.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the table path clear, accessories removed, docking interface visually intact, but the table continued to stop short of home position during controlled testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the MRI system from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the table behavior and error message, and escalated to Siemens service for table drive/docking evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient was on the table when the fault occurred
Exact table fault message or code
Whether the issue was docking, movement, vertical lift, or home-position recognition
Whether the table moved partially or not at all
Whether the fault occurred at the console, table-side controls, or both
Coils, pads, or accessories installed at the time
Indicator lights or system status messages
Unusual sound, drag, heat, smell, or vibration
Whether emergency stop or interlock conditions were active

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Aera MRI System - Gradient Cooling / Water Cooling / Chiller Temperature Fault
description: Troubleshooting Aera gradient cooling, water cooling, chiller temperature, flow, or heat-related scan interruption issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the cooling fault occurs during patient scanning, stop the exam if prompted by the system or if image acquisition cannot safely continue.

Remove the patient from the scanner if the fault is persistent, escalating, associated with system shutdown, or affecting table/scanner operation.

Expected outcome: Patient safety is maintained before technical troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Fault Message
steps.instructions:
Review the operator console, host messages, alarm history, or system event display for the exact wording of the fault.

Look for references to gradient cooling, water cooling, chiller temperature, water flow, supply temperature, return temperature, or cabinet cooling.

Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a cooling-related fault rather than a general host, gradient amplifier, or environmental warning.

steps.title: Check Room Temperature and Airflow
steps.instructions:
Verify the MRI equipment room and technical room temperatures are within the site’s normal operating range.

Check for blocked air vents, failed room HVAC, doors propped open, warm air recirculation, or recent facilities work.

Expected outcome: The room environment is stable and not causing the cooling system to operate outside normal limits.

If room HVAC is failed or the technical room is overheated, notify Facilities and stop scanning until temperature conditions are corrected.

steps.title: Inspect the Chiller for Active Alarms
steps.instructions:
Check the external chiller display, if accessible and allowed by site policy.

Look for high temperature, low flow, low water level, pump fault, compressor fault, phase/power fault, or filter restriction alarms.

Expected outcome: The chiller either shows normal operation or provides a visible fault that helps direct escalation.

If the chiller has an active alarm that cannot be cleared by normal site procedure, escalate to Facilities, Siemens service, or the chiller service vendor.

steps.title: Verify Chiller Power and Disconnect Status
steps.instructions:
Confirm the chiller is powered, running, and not disabled by an emergency stop, disconnect switch, breaker, or service lockout.

Do not reset breakers repeatedly. If a breaker trips again, stop and escalate.

Expected outcome: The chiller has stable power and is actively running.

steps.title: Check for Water Leaks or Visible Moisture
steps.instructions:
Inspect around the chiller, water lines, floor area, wall penetrations, and equipment room connections for leaks, pooling, condensation, or wet insulation.

Expected outcome: No visible leak or water damage is present.

If water is found, stop troubleshooting, protect the area, notify Facilities, and remove the MRI from service until evaluated.

steps.title: Check Water Level or Pressure Indicators
steps.instructions:
If the chiller has an external sight glass, level indicator, pressure gauge, or display reading, verify whether it appears normal for the site.

Do not add fluid unless this is part of your approved site procedure and the correct coolant mixture is known.

Expected outcome: Water level and pressure appear normal, or a low-level/pressure condition is identified for escalation.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Cooling Hoses and Connections
steps.instructions:
Visually inspect external hoses and quick connections for kinks, collapsed sections, disconnected fittings, strain, or signs of blockage.

Expected outcome: Cooling lines are connected, unrestricted, and not visibly damaged.

If a hose is kinked or restricted externally and can be safely corrected without disassembly, correct it and monitor the system.

steps.title: Check Chiller Filter or Strainer Status Externally
steps.instructions:
If the chiller has an accessible filter status indicator, service light, or differential pressure gauge, check for evidence of restriction.

Do not open sealed strainers or coolant circuits unless trained and authorized.

Expected outcome: No obvious restriction is indicated, or the chiller requires service.

steps.title: Allow the System to Stabilize After Heat Load
steps.instructions:
Determine whether the fault occurred after heavy scanning, long gradient-intensive sequences, high patient volume, or recent restart.

Allow the cooling system to recover if permitted by site policy and if no active leak, alarm, or safety condition exists.

Expected outcome: Temperature returns toward normal and the fault clears without repeated recurrence.

If the fault clears and does not return during controlled operation, document the event and monitor.

steps.title: Restart Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions:
If Siemens guidance, site procedure, or the system prompt allows it, perform a controlled system restart after confirming the chiller is running and no unsafe condition exists.

Avoid repeated restart attempts if the same temperature or flow fault returns.

Expected outcome: A temporary communication or monitoring fault clears, or the underlying cooling issue returns and requires escalation.

steps.title: Run a Limited Operational Check
steps.instructions:
After the fault clears, verify the scanner returns to ready status.

Confirm there are no active cooling alarms, no abnormal chiller sounds, no visible leaks, and no recurring temperature warnings.

Expected outcome: The MAGNETOM Aera remains stable before being released for clinical use.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Scanning With Repeated Cooling Faults
steps.instructions:
If the fault returns during scanning, QA, or standby operation, remove the MRI from clinical use.

Repeated cooling faults may indicate chiller failure, restricted flow, pump problems, sensor faults, coolant issues, or gradient cooling circuit problems.

Expected outcome: The scanner is not used in a condition that may risk scan interruption, equipment damage, or patient safety.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported a MAGNETOM Aera gradient cooling fault during scanning, with the system warning of elevated cooling temperature.
CCR.Cause: Chiller was running but displayed a high-temperature alarm, and the MRI technical room temperature was above normal due to HVAC failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed scanner from service, notified Facilities for room HVAC correction, contacted Siemens/chiller service for evaluation, and labeled the MRI Out of Service pending verification.

helpfulDetails: Exact cooling or temperature fault message
Time fault occurred and whether patient was scanning
Sequence or scan type in progress
Room temperature and technical room conditions
Chiller display status or alarm shown
Water level or pressure indication, if visible
Any visible leaks, condensation, or wet flooring
Unusual chiller noise, pump noise, heat, or smell
Whether the fault cleared after cooldown or restart
Whether the issue returned during QA or clinical scanning

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Aera MRI System - Image Artifact, Ghosting, Shading, or Failed QA Scan
description: Troubleshooting image artifacts, ghosting, shading, distortion, RF noise, or failed MRI QA scans before escalating for service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If artifacts or image quality problems occur during a patient exam, do not continue scanning if diagnostic quality is questionable.

Move the patient to another MRI system if clinically necessary and available.

Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on images from a system with unresolved quality concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Artifact or QA Failure
steps.instructions:
Review the reported issue with MRI staff and determine whether the problem occurred during:

Patient imaging
Daily QA
Phantom testing
A specific protocol
A specific coil
A specific table position
All exams

Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to a repeatable symptom instead of a vague image quality complaint.

steps.title: Check for External RF Interference
steps.instructions:
Inspect the MRI room for unauthorized or unusual items, including phones, smart watches, tablets, pumps, monitors, cables, tools, carts, or non-MR equipment.

Remove any questionable item from the scan room before repeating QA.

Expected outcome: If RF noise or artifact was caused by external interference, image quality improves after the item is removed.

If this resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify MRI Room Door and RF Shielding Conditions
steps.instructions:
Confirm the scanner room door fully closes and seals properly. Look for damaged door fingers, loose shielding contact points, or objects preventing full closure.

Expected outcome: The RF-shielded room is properly closed during scanning.

If poor door closure caused the artifact, correct the obstruction or report the door/shielding issue for repair.

steps.title: Check Coil Selection and Coil Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the correct coil is selected for the exam or QA scan. Inspect the coil connector, table connector area, and coil cable routing for obvious damage, looseness, or contamination.

Reseat the coil connection if safe and appropriate.

Expected outcome: The coil is properly recognized and connected before scanning.

If the artifact follows one coil, remove that coil from service and test with another known-good coil.

steps.title: Repeat the Scan With a Known-Good Coil
steps.instructions:
If the issue occurred with a patient coil, repeat a basic test or QA sequence using a known-good coil approved for the system.

Expected outcome: If the known-good coil produces normal images, the original coil or coil cable is suspect.

If this resolves the issue, remove the faulty coil from service and document the coil-specific failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Phantom and QA Setup
steps.instructions:
For failed QA scans, verify the correct phantom is being used, positioned correctly, and filled according to site practice.

Check for phantom bubbles, leaks, incorrect orientation, poor centering, or movement during scanning.

Expected outcome: QA is repeated with a correctly positioned and stable phantom.

If the QA scan passes after correcting phantom setup, document the setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Verify Table Position and Patient/Phantom Centering
steps.instructions:
Confirm the patient or phantom is properly centered at isocenter and positioned according to the intended protocol.

Poor centering can contribute to shading, signal drop-off, distortion, or failed uniformity results.

Expected outcome: The scan is repeated with proper positioning and improved image quality.

steps.title: Check Protocol and Parameter Selection
steps.instructions:
Confirm MRI staff used the correct protocol, coil combination, field of view, slice position, acceleration settings, and scan orientation.

Compare the failed scan against a recently successful protocol if available.

Expected outcome: The issue is not caused by an incorrect or modified protocol.

If a protocol change caused the artifact, restore the correct protocol or involve the MRI lead/physicist as appropriate.

steps.title: Look for Patient Motion or Physiologic Ghosting
steps.instructions:
For patient images, check whether ghosting aligns with phase direction and whether motion, breathing, swallowing, cardiac motion, or patient discomfort may have contributed.

Expected outcome: Motion-related artifacts are identified as exam-specific rather than system failure.

If motion caused the issue, document it as clinical scan behavior, not equipment failure.

steps.title: Check for Metal or External Objects Near the Anatomy
steps.instructions:
Confirm there were no removable metal objects, clothing components, patches, ECG leads, jewelry, hair accessories, or external devices near the scan area.

Expected outcome: Artifact from external objects is ruled out before suspecting system hardware.

If removing the item resolves the artifact, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes Around the Scanner
steps.instructions:
Ask whether any of the following changed recently:

New equipment near the MRI suite
Construction nearby
Electrical work
HVAC work
RF door repair
Coil replacement
Software updates
Protocol changes
Failed chiller or cooling alerts

Expected outcome: The artifact is correlated with a recent environmental, facility, or system change.

steps.title: Check for System Warnings or Related Error Messages
steps.instructions:
Review the operator console for active warnings, coil messages, gradient warnings, cooling alerts, reconstruction errors, or QA-related messages.

Expected outcome: Any associated system warning is captured before escalation.

Do not clear or ignore recurring errors without documenting them.

steps.title: Repeat Daily QA Under Controlled Conditions
steps.instructions:
If no obvious external issue is found, repeat the required QA scan using the correct phantom, proper setup, correct coil, and standard site procedure.

Expected outcome: QA either passes, confirming the issue was setup-related, or fails again, confirming a repeatable problem.

If QA fails repeatedly under controlled conditions, remove the system from clinical use according to site policy.

steps.title: Compare Artifact Behavior Across Coils and Sequences
steps.instructions:
Determine whether the problem occurs:

With one coil only
With all coils
On one protocol only
On all sequences
In one image plane
At one table position
Only during high-SAR or demanding sequences

Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering can provide Siemens service or the physicist with a clear failure pattern.

steps.title: Escalate When Image Quality Cannot Be Verified
steps.instructions:
If image artifacts persist, QA fails repeatedly, or diagnostic image quality is uncertain, stop troubleshooting at the external-check level.

Expected outcome: The system is removed from clinical use until qualified service evaluation, physicist review, or manufacturer support confirms safe operation.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported ghosting and shading on MAGNETOM Aera images, with the daily phantom QA scan failing uniformity.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the QA phantom was not centered correctly and a non-MR accessory cart had been left near the scan room doorway.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the accessory cart, repositioned the phantom at isocenter, repeated QA successfully, and returned the MRI system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred during patient imaging or QA
Room door fully closed and sealed
Any unusual equipment found in or near the MRI room
Whether QA passed after repeat testing

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Aera MRI System - Host, syngo, reconstruction, or system boot fault
description: Troubleshooting Aera host startup, syngo software, reconstruction, or boot-related faults before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is inside the bore and no exam is actively in progress before troubleshooting host, console, or reconstruction issues. Expected outcome: The MRI system is safe to evaluate without interrupting an active scan or delaying patient removal. If a patient is involved, stop troubleshooting and coordinate safe patient removal through MRI staff.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Ask MRI staff what failed and when it happened: Host will not boot; syngo interface frozen; Reconstruction delayed or failed; Exam will not load; System stuck during startup; Scan completed but images are missing; Error appeared after shutdown, restart, power event, or software freeze. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to host startup, application behavior, reconstruction, image transfer, or full system availability.

steps.title: Check Console Power and External Power Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the host computer, monitors, keyboard, mouse, and console peripherals have power. Check for: Monitor power lights; Host power indicator; UPS status, if present; Tripped power strip or switched outlet; Loose power cable behind the console. Expected outcome: Console hardware is powered normally. If the issue is resolved by restoring external power or reconnecting a loose cable, verify normal startup and stop.

steps.title: Check for Monitor or Display-Only Issues
steps.instructions: If the system appears down, confirm whether the host is actually running but the display is blank. Check: Monitor input/source selection; Video cable connection; Display power; Multiple monitors, if equipped; Whether keyboard lights respond. Expected outcome: A blank screen is not mistaken for a failed host. If the host is running and only the display connection was loose or misconfigured, correct it, verify syngo display, and stop.

steps.title: Check Keyboard, Mouse, and Console Input Devices
steps.instructions: Confirm the keyboard and mouse are connected and responsive. Look for: Loose USB connections; Damaged cables; Nonresponsive mouse or keyboard; Accidental disconnection during cleaning or console movement. Expected outcome: The operator can interact with the host and syngo environment. If input devices were disconnected or faulty, reconnect or replace externally, verify normal operation, and stop.

steps.title: Verify Whether the Issue Is a Frozen Application or Full Host Lockup
steps.instructions: Determine whether only syngo is frozen or the entire computer is nonresponsive. Check whether: Mouse pointer moves; Keyboard shortcuts respond; Clock updates; Other windows respond; Error messages can be acknowledged. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering can distinguish software freeze from full host failure. If the host is partially responsive, coordinate with MRI staff and follow site-approved restart procedures. Do not force repeated restarts without documenting the behavior.

steps.title: Confirm Reconstruction Queue or Image Availability
steps.instructions: If scans completed but images are delayed or missing, ask staff whether the issue affects: One patient only; One protocol or sequence; All studies; Only recon images; Image transfer to PACS; Local viewing on the scanner. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into reconstruction delay, local image issue, or network/PACS transfer issue. If images are present locally but not in PACS, troubleshoot network/export workflow separately rather than assuming reconstruction failure.

steps.title: Check Network and External Communication Indicators
steps.instructions: For host or image transfer complaints, verify external network conditions before escalating. Check: Network cable seated at console connection point; Link lights at accessible network jack or switch port, if available; Whether other modality network functions are affected; Whether PACS, RIS, or worklist access is also failing. Expected outcome: A network interruption is ruled in or ruled out as the cause of missing worklist, PACS transfer, or archive behavior. If a loose network cable or known network outage is found, document and route appropriately.

steps.title: Ask About Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the fault started after: Power outage; Emergency power testing; Planned shutdown; Software update; Network maintenance; HVAC or cooling issue; Service visit; Unexpected system restart. Expected outcome: The fault is tied to a possible external event or recent service condition. This helps avoid treating a site power or network event as a random host failure.

steps.title: Check Room and Equipment Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the scanner equipment area is not showing obvious environmental problems. Check for: High room temperature; Unusual heat at console or equipment cabinets; Burning smell; Abnormal fan noise; Water leak near equipment; Alarm indicators on nearby support equipment. Expected outcome: Obvious environmental or safety concerns are identified before further restart attempts. If heat, smoke smell, water, or abnormal equipment behavior is present, stop and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Perform One Controlled Restart Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the system is not actively scanning, no patient is involved, and site procedure allows it, coordinate with MRI staff before performing a controlled restart. Expected outcome: The host, syngo environment, and reconstruction function return normally after a proper restart. If the system boots normally and MRI staff confirm scan/recon operation, document the restart and final status. Do not perform repeated power cycles if the same fault returns.

steps.title: Verify System Readiness After Recovery
steps.instructions: After any successful restart or correction, have MRI staff confirm: syngo opens normally; Patient browser is accessible; Protocols load; Reconstruction completes; Images display locally; Worklist/PACS function is normal, if applicable; No active system error remains. Expected outcome: The device is returned to service only after functional readiness is confirmed. If the issue returns during verification, remove the system from service and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported the Siemens MAGNETOM Aera syngo host would not complete startup and reconstruction was unavailable for scheduled exams.
CCR.Cause: Verified console power, monitor connections, keyboard/mouse, network connection, and environmental conditions; fault persisted after one controlled restart.
CCR.Resolution: Removed MRI system from service, labeled Out of Service, documented startup behavior, and escalated to Siemens service for host/reconstruction system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or startup screen observed
Whether syngo froze or the entire host locked up
Whether PACS, worklist, or network functions were affected
Network cable/link light status, if checked
Any recent power outage, shutdown, update, or service visit
Unusual heat, smell, noise, or room temperature issue
Whether one controlled restart was attempted

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI System - RF Coil Overload / Coil Recognition / Coil Communication Fault
description: Troubleshooting RF coil overload, coil not recognized, coil element dropout, or coil communication faults on Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI systems before escalating to MRI service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the coil fault occurs during patient setup or scanning, stop the exam only when it is safe to do so. Remove the patient from the scanner before troubleshooting coils, table connectors, or RF accessories. Expected outcome: The patient is safe, and troubleshooting is performed without risk of patient injury or repeat exposure to a known equipment fault.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Coil Fault
steps.instructions: Review the message shown at the MRI host or operator console. Identify whether the issue is: RF coil overload; Coil not recognized; Coil ID error; Coil element dropout; Coil communication failure; Fault only with one specific coil or scan protocol. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is understood before parts are swapped or the issue is escalated.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Coil Is Selected and Properly Positioned
steps.instructions: Confirm the coil being used matches the anatomy and selected exam. Check that the coil is seated correctly around the patient area and is not twisted, folded, pinched, or placed incorrectly. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the intended coil and the error clears. If this resolves the issue, document the positioning correction and stop.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Coil Cable or Connector Problems
steps.instructions: Inspect the coil cable, connector shell, strain relief, and visible pins or contact areas. Look for: Bent or damaged connector pins; Cracked connector housing; Loose strain relief; Cable kinks or compression damage; Fluid contamination; Signs of overheating or odor. Expected outcome: No visible damage or contamination is present. If damage, fluid intrusion, burning smell, or heat is found, remove the coil from service and escalate.

steps.title: Reseat the Coil Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the RF coil at the appropriate table or interface connection point. Ensure the connector is fully seated and latched if applicable. Expected outcome: A loose or partially seated coil connection is corrected, and the system recognizes the coil. If this resolves the issue, perform a functional check before returning the coil to service.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Compatible Coil
steps.instructions: Use a known-good coil that is compatible with the Skyra and the same exam type, when available. Do not force connectors or use incompatible coils. Expected outcome: If the known-good coil works, the original coil is suspect. If the known-good coil also fails, the issue may involve the table interface, coil port, RF chain, or system communication.

steps.title: Test the Suspect Coil on Another Compatible Connection or Setup
steps.instructions: If safe and available, try the suspect coil on another compatible port, table position, or approved configuration. Expected outcome: If the coil fails in multiple valid setups, the coil is likely faulty. If the fault follows one table connector or interface location, the system-side connection may be suspect.

steps.title: Check for Coil Overload Conditions
steps.instructions: For RF coil overload messages, confirm the patient setup and protocol are appropriate. Check for: Incorrect coil selection; Patient positioned too close to the coil housing or cable path; Coil cable looped or routed improperly; Excessive SAR protocol selection; Metallic objects, ECG leads, monitoring cables, or conductive loops near the RF field; Coil padding or accessories placed incorrectly. Expected outcome: External setup conditions that can contribute to RF overload are corrected. If the warning clears after correcting setup, repeat only an appropriate safety-verified test before returning to clinical use.

steps.title: Inspect Table Coil Interfaces and Docking Area
steps.instructions: With the patient removed and the system safe to access, inspect the table coil connection area. Look for: Debris in connector areas; Damaged contacts; Loose table interface panels; Evidence of fluid contamination; Table positioning or docking issues that may affect communication. Expected outcome: No visible system-side connector issue is found. If contamination, damage, or table interface abnormality is found, remove the affected setup from service and escalate.

steps.title: Check Whether the Fault Is Protocol-Specific
steps.instructions: Ask the technologist whether the issue occurs: On every sequence; Only on high-SAR sequences; Only on a specific anatomy protocol; Only with one patient setup; Only after the coil has been used for a period of time. Expected outcome: A repeatable pattern is identified. If only one protocol causes overload warnings, involve MRI applications support or Siemens service before repeated clinical use.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the coil is properly connected, no damage is seen, and the issue appears to be a temporary communication fault, perform a controlled system restart according to site procedure. Do not repeatedly reboot as a substitute for troubleshooting a recurring coil fault. Expected outcome: A temporary communication fault clears and does not return during verification.

steps.title: Perform a Verification Scan or Approved QA Check
steps.instructions: After any correction, perform an approved coil check, phantom scan, or site-specific MRI QA test. Confirm: Coil is recognized; Expected coil elements are active; No overload warning appears; Image quality is acceptable; No abnormal heat, odor, or cable movement is present. Expected outcome: The coil and system pass verification before being returned to clinical service.

CCR.Complaint: MRI technologist reported that the Siemens MAGNETOM Skyra displayed a coil communication fault and would not recognize the selected body array coil.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the coil connector was not fully seated at the table interface; no visible cable damage, contamination, heat, or connector damage was observed.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the coil connector, verified proper coil recognition, completed an approved test scan without recurrence, and returned the coil/system to service.

helpfulDetails: Coil name and serial number if available
Exam type or protocol being used
Whether fault was overload, recognition, element dropout, or communication related
Whether the issue followed the coil or stayed with the table/interface
Whether another compatible coil was tested
Whether abnormal heat, smell, fluid, or visible damage was present
Whether a phantom, QA, or verification scan passed

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI System - Patient table movement, docking, or drive fault
description: Troubleshooting MAGNETOM Skyra patient table movement, docking, positioning, or drive-related faults before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If a patient is on the table and movement is abnormal, delayed, noisy, or unavailable, stop table operation immediately. Communicate with MRI staff and assist with safe patient removal using site-approved MRI safety procedures. Expected outcome: The patient is not moved using a table system that may be unreliable.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Table Fault
steps.instructions: Ask staff what occurred: Table will not move in or out; Table movement stops unexpectedly; Docking does not complete; Table position is incorrect; Drive motor noise is abnormal; Table movement fault appears at the host; Emergency stop was pressed. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Verify MRI Room Safety Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that the scan room is safe to enter and that all tools, test items, and personal items are MRI-safe or kept outside Zone IV as required. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not introduce projectile or safety risk.

steps.title: Check for Patient, Accessory, or Obstruction Interference
steps.instructions: Inspect the table path, dock area, side rails, coil cables, pads, straps, sheets, and patient accessories for anything blocking movement. Expected outcome: The table path and docking area are clear. If an obstruction is found and removed, retest table movement. If normal operation returns, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Table Lock Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that any emergency stop, table stop, or movement inhibit condition has been cleared according to site procedure. Confirm with MRI staff whether an emergency stop was activated during the event. Expected outcome: Table movement is not being blocked by a safety stop condition.

steps.title: Verify Table Docking Position
steps.instructions: Inspect whether the patient table is fully seated, aligned, and properly docked at the magnet interface. Look for partial docking, uneven table position, or physical misalignment. Expected outcome: The table is mechanically aligned before drive movement is tested.

steps.title: Check Table Communication and Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe table control indicators, system messages, and host console alerts during a controlled movement attempt. Note whether the system recognizes the table, reports a docking fault, or shows a drive/positioning error. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to movement, docking, recognition, or position feedback.

steps.title: Test Movement Without a Patient
steps.instructions: With MRI staff approval, test table movement in a controlled condition with no patient present. Use normal table controls and observe for: No movement; Jerky or uneven motion; Unexpected stopping; Abnormal grinding or clicking; Excessive resistance; Host position mismatch. Expected outcome: The table moves smoothly through the expected range without abnormal sound or error. If movement is restored and no errors return, document the finding and return the unit only after operational checks meet site policy.

steps.title: Check External Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible table-related cables, docking contacts, and external connection points for looseness, damage, debris, fluid contamination, or strain. Do not force connectors or disassemble internal table components. Expected outcome: External communication and power paths appear secure and undamaged.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Fluid Contamination
steps.instructions: Check the table surface, base, dock area, drive track area, and accessible exterior panels for signs of impact, cracks, bent components, fluid intrusion, or unusual residue. Expected outcome: No visible damage or contamination is present that could affect movement.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Heat, Smell, or Noise
steps.instructions: During limited testing, stop immediately if there is burning smell, unusual heat, repeated clicking, grinding, or motor strain. Expected outcome: The table does not show signs of drive motor, brake, power, or mechanical failure during external troubleshooting.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If MRI operations allow, have authorized MRI staff perform a controlled system/table reset or restart according to site procedure. Do not interrupt scanning operations or restart the system without MRI staff coordination. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication faults clear after restart. If the table fault clears and controlled movement checks pass, document the event and final status.

steps.title: Verify Final Operational Status
steps.instructions: Before returning the system to use, confirm: Table docks correctly; Table moves in and out smoothly; Position readout behaves normally; No repeated alarms or host messages occur; No abnormal noise, heat, smell, or resistance is present. Expected outcome: The MAGNETOM Skyra table is safe and reliable for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: MRI staff reported the Siemens MAGNETOM Skyra patient table would not dock fully and displayed a table movement fault at the console.
CCR.Cause: Found the docking area obstructed by a coil cable routed across the table interface, preventing full table seating.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the obstruction, verified table docking and in/out movement without a patient, confirmed no further table movement faults, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the table was occupied when the fault occurred
Exact host or console error message
Whether the table would move in, out, both, or neither direction
Docking status before and after troubleshooting
Emergency stop or table stop status
Coil cables, pads, sheets, or accessories found in the table path
Any abnormal sound, vibration, heat, smell, or resistance

title: Siemens Healthineers MAGNETOM Skyra MRI System - Gradient Amplifier, Cooling, or Temperature Warning
description: Troubleshooting gradient, cooling, or temperature warnings by checking room conditions, airflow, external cooling indicators, and safe escalation points.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the MAGNETOM Skyra displays a gradient amplifier, cooling, or temperature warning during patient scanning, stop scanning only when clinically safe and follow MRI department policy.

If the scan cannot safely continue, remove the patient from the scanner and place the system out of clinical use.

Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before equipment troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Warning or Fault Behavior
steps.instructions:
Review the operator console message and document the exact wording, error code, time of occurrence, and whether the warning appeared during startup, idle state, prescan, or active scanning.

Check whether the issue is intermittent or repeats immediately after system reset.

Expected outcome: The failure pattern is clear enough to separate environmental issues from likely internal gradient or cooling faults.

steps.title: Check Room Temperature and HVAC Conditions
steps.instructions:
Verify the MRI equipment room, scanner room, and technical room are within the facility’s required environmental range.

Look for signs of poor HVAC performance, blocked vents, unusually warm rooms, open doors, or recent facilities work.

Expected outcome: The system is not overheating due to room temperature or poor air circulation.

If room temperature is high, contact Facilities/HVAC and do not continue scanning until environmental conditions are corrected.

steps.title: Inspect External Cooling System Indicators
steps.instructions:
Check external cooling cabinet, chiller, heat exchanger, or facility water system indicators where accessible without opening restricted panels.

Look for alarm lights, abnormal display readings, tripped local disconnects, leaks, low flow indications, or unusual pump noise.

Expected outcome: Cooling support equipment appears powered, stable, and free of obvious external alarms.

steps.title: Check for Water Leaks or Condensation
steps.instructions:
Inspect accessible areas around cooling lines, cabinets, floor drains, and nearby mechanical connections.

Do not touch exposed liquid near electrical equipment.

Expected outcome: No visible leak, pooling water, or condensation condition is found.

If liquid is present, remove the system from service and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify Power and Local Disconnect Status
steps.instructions:
Confirm that visible power indicators are normal on accessible support equipment.

Check for any facility breaker, emergency power event, UPS alarm, or recent power interruption reported by staff.

Expected outcome: The gradient and cooling support systems have stable external power.

steps.title: Check Airflow Around Cabinets and Equipment
steps.instructions:
Inspect the technical room for blocked vents, stored items against cabinets, clogged exterior grilles, or objects restricting airflow.

Remove only external obstructions that can be safely cleared.

Expected outcome: Heat-generating cabinets have adequate clearance and ventilation.

steps.title: Ask Staff About Recent Workflow Changes
steps.instructions:
Determine whether the warning appears after long high-duty-cycle protocols, repeated advanced scans, or heavy back-to-back usage.

Gradient temperature warnings can appear when system demand exceeds cooling recovery.

Expected outcome: You identify whether the issue is related to scan load or occurs under normal use.

steps.title: Allow Safe Cooldown if Appropriate
steps.instructions:
If the warning appears to be temperature-related and the system is otherwise stable, allow the scanner and support equipment time to cool according to site policy.

Do not override warnings or continue scanning patients on an unstable system.

Expected outcome: The warning clears after cooldown if the cause was temporary thermal load.

steps.title: Restart Only if Safe and Allowed by Site Procedure
steps.instructions:
If no patient is present, no liquid or electrical hazard exists, and local procedure allows, perform a controlled system restart through normal operator workflow.

Do not repeatedly cycle power or reset major MRI support equipment without vendor guidance.

Expected outcome: A temporary communication or thermal status warning may clear after a normal restart.

steps.title: Run Only Approved Local Checks
steps.instructions:
If the warning clears, perform only approved site-level checks such as idle observation, console status review, or routine QA workflow permitted by the MRI department.

Watch for repeated gradient, cooling, or temperature warnings.

Expected outcome: The system remains stable before return to clinical use.

steps.title: Stop Troubleshooting if the Warning Returns
steps.instructions:
If the warning returns, worsens, appears with abnormal noise, visible leak, overheating smell, cabinet alarm, or failed QA, remove the MRI from service.

Expected outcome: A likely internal gradient amplifier, cooling loop, sensor, flow, or control fault is escalated appropriately.
